  • Late-day scheduled watchlist run. Full sweep of all 50 watchlist profiles after the evening run. Today is a heavy ICA Future of FinCrime & Compliance Summit / Engage Employee Summit day — multiple watchlist members posting recaps from both events.

Aisling Twomey (ICA paddle session, 4h), Emma Williams (FCA AML panel, 1d), Alasdair Fraser (ICA strongly-agree panel, 7h, AND HMICFRS NCA inspection teardown, 1d — capped at 2 per the rules), Katy McIntyre (embarrassment as underexplored emotion, 2d), Katy Busby (redundancy posts subtle-relief framing, 1d), Stuart Martin (Lloyds Safety Game Changers event, 3h — new since the evening Game Changers AgenticAI card), Janina Norton (AXA Engage Employee listening-data panel, 8h), Jane van Zyl (IPPR / Action for Children in-work child poverty 44%→72%, 1d).

Larissa Schneider-Kim (`larissa-schneider-kim`) returned an empty activity feed — either no recent original activity or page failed to render the feed components on this load.

Adds to the AML / FinCrime cluster already running this week via Chantelle Obatolu, Emma Williams and Adrianna Fabijanska — the watchlist is genuinely showing the rhythm of this conference circuit, useful for timing.
  • Cross-pattern: Engage Employee Summit clustering. Five separate watchlist members posted Engage Employee Summit recaps today across the evening and late runs (Sam Bleazard, Siobhan Heffron, Megan Yawor, Jennifer Sproul, Stuart Martin, Janina Norton, Kaylee Darkins, Nicola Marshall) — pattern is loud enough to warrant a named-account scan or a follow-up Engage 2026 keyword sweep next run.
  • Infra note — surfacing loudly per the skill's "infra failures should be loud" rule: SKILL.md still describes the old infrastructure where watchlist cards live in `watchlist.html` with a `<!-- NEWEST-DAY-INSERT-BELOW -->` marker. That marker no longer exists in `watchlist.html` — the page has been refactored to fetch `index.html` and filter cards by `data-audience="watchlist"` (see the `loadWatchlist()` / `extractWatchlistCardsFromHtml()` functions in watchlist.html). Watchlist cards are now written into `index.html` alongside the keyword-search audiences, which is what the 2026-05-20 (evening) run also did. This late run follows that same pattern. Worth updating SKILL.md the next time Toby is in the loop — the "do not touch index.html from this job" instruction now contradicts the actual infrastructure.

1 reaction · 1 comment · 0 reposts
Post age
4h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Really useful framing. The completion percentage on a compliance video tells you almost nothing about whether the learner could actually do the thing the policy describes, the engagement pattern at least gives you a signal worth interrogating. How are you seeing teams act on those patterns once they spot them?
Connection request
Hi Rachel, really useful post on the interpretation gap between engagement data and actual capability. Would love to swap notes on how you're getting L&D teams to act on those patterns rather than just ticking completion.

Run notes — 2026-05-19

  • Watchlist scan executed first across the named list. Four qualifying new posts surfaced: Chantelle Obatolu (ABN AMRO deepfake escalation, fresh original post), Emma Williams (short FCA-legal-profession share), Kevin Green (First Bus People function awayday, 26/27 planning), Nick Holmes (Avalere Health learning redesign).
  • Watchlist dedup against last 7 days removed: Adrianna Fabijanska (only recent activity is a non-commentary repost on Bitcoin / AI, out of domain); Aisling Twomey (recent activity is reposts with no commentary); Ben Westwood (5d "never be a public speaker" post already logged 2026-05-17); Dane Pedro (recent posts already logged on 2026-05-17); Simon Fanshawe (CIPD Festival inclusive leadership post already logged 2026-05-18); Stella Gavinho (Random Acts of Wellness pain-points post already logged 2026-05-18). Sam Bleazard had a 3d original post on storytelling but content already drifting from ICP relevance, skipped.
  • Remaining watchlist coverage abbreviated this run to keep the run inside its time envelope while keyword discovery still got attention. Named profiles not scanned this pass will be picked up in the next 1-2 sessions; no high-volume posters likely missed based on prior-run posting cadence.
  • Keyword discovery yield was thin. Audience A and B searches were dominated by US/Asia/Middle-East content, vendor blog spam (Compliance Consultant UK, Zinc, The AI Consultancy, Psychosocial Safety Institute) and below-threshold consultant promo. UK-practitioner-language searches ("reasonable steps" + employer, "difficult conversations" + manager, "psychosocial" + leader) all returned 0-1 viable candidates after deduping and ICP filtering.
  • Strongest keyword pick: Kim Smart on FCA Year 2 Consumer Duty Board reports. SMF 16/17 holder, board-level role, 1st degree connection. Directly on ICP. Slightly older post (2w originally, recent edit pulled it into the past-week filter) but the angle is sharp.
  • Audience A: only one strong candidate (Rachel W. on engagement-data-vs-completion theatre). Strong content, low engagement on her side, but the framing lands exactly where Real Talk Studio's pitch lives.
  • Audience C: Susannah Donaldson's pre-conference push for Pinsent Masons clears the 25-reaction threshold and seeds Track-2 comment-mining potential after the conference itself.
  • Patrick Brodie's NFM mini-series post resurfaced in the COCON search but is already logged (2026-05-17). Re-confirmed dedup.
  • No active-buyer (Path 5) shopping-language posts surfaced this run.
  • Total cards: 7 (4 watchlist + 1 CHRO + 1 ICP + 1 Influencer). Below the 6-9 keyword-search candidate target because UK practitioner-language searches went dry across multiple paths this run; quality kept over quantity per the skill brief.

2026-05-18 (Monday) — Monday morning scan. Watchlist scan executed first across all 50 named profiles (the expanded list landed Friday). Five qualifying watchlist posts surfaced: Chantelle Obatolu (54m ago, brand-new AML Unfiltered launch — an immersive AML investigation simulation she built in response to DM requests for work experience, lands DIRECTLY on Real Talk Studio thesis territory), Kevin Green (1d, First Bus CPO — Engage Employee speaker promo with substantive halved-attrition / 40% absence reduction stats, 60 reactions), Simon Fanshawe (2d, CIPD Festival of Work main-stage speaker promo on inclusive leadership and the trade-off responsibility frame), Stella Gavinho (2d, FTSE 100 Global Head of Wellbeing — sharp "random acts of wellness" vs real pain points framing, Engage Employee Summit), Megan Yawor (5d, L&D designer — "Ideas Shouldn't Need Permission" on cross-functional contribution), Nick Holmes (4d, organisational behaviour PhD researcher — Manchester People @Power Summit panel on "Mandating Change — Is compliance the answer to race equity?", lands the policy-vs-cultural-change frame). Remaining 44 watchlist profiles were dry, housekeeping (mostly ICA Future of FinCrime Summit speaker promos from the AML cluster — Adrianna, Aisling, Alasdair, Samuel, Scott Mitchell, Alan Campbell, Kostyantyn already logged or housekeeping), pure reposts (Miloš, Ian Tomney-Bell, Larissa Schneider-Kim), light personal content (Sam Bleazard Fortnum's team party, Joel Turner Betfred internal comms reshare, Katy Busby exam prep personal reflection, Joyce Idoniboye podcast recap), or already-logged duplicates (Emma Williams curiosity / AI era already 2026-05-17-evening, Ben Westwood 15-years-ago public speaker already 2026-05-17, Dane Pedro mental wellness leadership already 2026-05-17). Keyword side: ran ~10 past-week searches across Path 1 (Worker Protection Act all reasonable steps, NFM culture, tick box compliance), Path 2 (first time manager freeze, knowing-doing gap), Path 3 (none productive), Path 4 (none productive), Path 5 (any recommendations manager training — dry as usual), plus Audience B Track-1 (Consumer Duty vulnerable customers outcomes, reasonable steps evidence) and Audience C influencer modifiers (leadership programme didn't stick / transfer). Practitioner desert pattern continues into Monday morning — Worker Protection cluster only returned Heeral Gudka briefing paper already logged + Fiona McPhail (below threshold, repeat from prior runs); knowing-doing gap returned Eileen Patterson and Anup Thapa (single-digit engagement); Consumer Duty cluster duplicated Sarah Logeswaran (already 2026-05-15-evening); tick box compliance returned only Kahoot and SECMON1 vendor noise; reasonable steps cluster duplicated Heeral via Lorraine Rew reshare. Best keyword hits: Rachel Clelland (Finance Director associated with Self Space wellbeing consultancy — 2d, 7 reactions — sharp NFM-as-financial-risk frame with hidden drag / culture risk on P&L language) routed to Audience B/Compliance ICP. John Whitfield (58m, 6 reactions — behavioural science consultant, second post this week on training transfer using Harper et al 2025 Substance / Structure / Relationships framing — different from his 2026-05-15-evening logged post on meta-analyses) routed to Audience C influencer. Jeff Pierce (5d, "Fixing weak accountability in new managers" consultant — first-time manager freeze frame using Jocko Willink / Marcus Aurelius) routed to Audience C influencer. Pattern flag: weekday Monday morning keyword cluster yield is also thinning — six runs in a row producing 1-3 keyword candidates at most, the watchlist-first switchover is doing the heavy lifting. Recommendation for next run: lean even harder on Track-2 comment-mining underneath any law firm or Big-4 post that clears 50+ reactions, since both Patrick Brodie and Will Chalk posts from 2026-05-17 have likely accumulated more practitioner commenters worth surfacing now.

5. Megan Yawor

Watchlist — L&D essay on cross-functional contribution: the biggest barrier to improvement isn't bad ideas, it's the belief that only certain people are allowed to improve them; ideas outside someone's remit get read as overstepping
Watchlist TODO
Role
Talent · L&D · EX · Award-Winning Designer with a passion for driving people-focused change (see profile)
Source
Watchlist scan
Post topic
Strong work depends on collaboration and idea-sharing, but the biggest barrier to improvement often isn't bad ideas — it's the belief that only certain people are allowed to improve them. Ideas that sit outside someone's immediate remit get read as either helpful insight or overstepping. If we want genuinely connected experiences built by and for our people, cross-functional contribution has to be expected and welcomed, not mistaken for intrusion. The strongest ideas rarely come from within a single team.
Engagement
several reactions, comments active
Post age
5d
Link
View activity
Draft comment
Really useful read. The overstepping read is what kills most of the cross-functional ideas before anyone even gets to evaluate them on merit. How have you found teams shift the default from intrusion to invited?
Connection request
Already connected — skip

7. Rachel Clelland

Compliance ICP — reframes NFM as a formal financial risk: toxic workplace pattern is the "hidden drag" (manager time on people issues, brilliant talent silently quitting, teams going silent) that rarely lands on P&L until too late, with FCA rules putting behaviour firmly under conduct & governance
ICP TODO
Role
Finance Director (associated with Self Space — wellbeing & culture consultancy)
Post topic
"Non-financial misconduct is now a formal financial risk." The toxic workplace, the bullying, harassment, or "difficult" behaviour everyone works around because someone is "too valuable" or has been there too long. From a finance perspective culture risk rarely lands neatly on a P&L until it's too late — shows up as hidden drag: managers sorting out people issues instead of focusing on workloads, brilliant talent silently quitting, teams going silent until the truth comes out fast. FCA's incoming rules make it clear that behaviour now sits firmly under conduct and governance. Not a wellbeing initiative or nice-to-have — risk management, value protection, performance infrastructure.
Engagement
7 reactions · 1 comment
Post age
2d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Really useful frame. The hidden drag bit is what most boards underestimate, the cost shows up months before the tribunal does. What have you found actually moves it from a P&L footnote into a real risk conversation?
Connection request
Hi Rachel, really useful post on NFM as a formal financial risk. Would love to swap notes on what's actually surfacing the hidden drag for finance teams before the tribunal stage.

8. John Whitfield MBA

Influencer / behavioural science consultant — Harper et al. 2025 train-the-trainer paper: most programmes fail because organisations focus on transferring knowledge while underestimating systems and relationships; needs Substance + Structure + Relationships working together
Influencer TODO
Role
Applying Behavioural Science to Real World Performance (independent consultant)
Post topic
"The biggest weakness in most training strategies is not the content. It is the assumption that knowledge automatically transfers into behaviour." A recent paper (Harper et al., 2025) on large-scale train-the-trainer professional development argues most programmes fail because organisations focus on transferring knowledge while underestimating systems and relationships. Three conditions needed: Substance (knowledge clear, codified, usable), Structure (processes, support, delivery designed properly), Relationships (trust, collaboration, psychological safety for learning to transfer into practice). The same programme produces very different outcomes depending on leadership and context.
Engagement
6 reactions · 1 comment
Post age
1h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Really useful read, this. The Relationships condition is the one most programmes treat as the soft layer and then can't explain why the behaviour didn't shift. How are you seeing teams actually test for it before they invest in the next cohort?
Connection request
Hi John, really useful framing on the Harper et al Substance / Structure / Relationships piece. Would love to swap notes on how you're seeing teams actually evidence the Relationships condition before the cohort kicks off.

9. Jeff Pierce

Influencer / consultant on new manager accountability — if you've promoted someone into their first management role without teaching them how to respond under pressure, you haven't set them up to lead, you've set them up to freeze
Influencer TODO
Role
6x Dad · Fixing weak accountability in new managers (independent consultant)
Post topic
Frames a leadership lesson around Jocko Willink's "Good" response (every obstacle becomes part of the plan) and Marcus Aurelius's "the obstacle is the way". Argues first-time managers are defined not by what they say in the prepared all-hands but by how they respond when the deal falls apart or the timeline slips. If you've promoted someone into a first management role without teaching them how to respond under pressure, you haven't set them up to lead — you've set them up to freeze.
Engagement
multi-reaction, 5d post still in feed
Post age
5d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Sharp read. The freeze isn't a knowledge gap, it's a rehearsal gap, the all-hands script doesn't survive the first deal slipping. How are you seeing people actually build the under-pressure rep before the promotion lands?
Connection request
Hi Jeff, sharp read on the first-time manager freeze under pressure. Would love to swap notes on what's actually working to build the under-pressure rep before someone gets promoted into the role.

Remaining 44 watchlist profiles were dry, housekeeping, pure reposts, or already-logged duplicates.
  • Keyword side (~10 searches): Worker Protection Act / NFM culture / first time manager freeze / knowing-doing gap / Path 5 any recommendations manager training / Consumer Duty vulnerable customers / reasonable steps evidence / tick box compliance / leadership programme didn't stick or transfer. Six runs in a row producing 1-3 keyword candidates only — weekday Monday morning is now showing the same drought pattern as weekends.
  • Best keyword candidates worth logging: Rachel Clelland (3rd+, Finance Director / Self Space, NFM as financial risk + hidden drag frame — routed to ICP), John Whitfield (2nd, behavioural science consultant, second post this week on training transfer — Harper et al Substance / Structure / Relationships framing, routed to Influencer; different from his 2026-05-15-evening logged meta-analyses post), Jeff Pierce (2nd, new manager accountability consultant, first-time manager freeze frame, routed to Influencer).
  • Dedup hits this run: Adrianna Fabijanska (FCA Working Together to Fight Financial Crime — already 2026-05-17), Emma Williams (curiosity / AI era — already 2026-05-17-evening), Chantelle Obatolu (UK digital ID 2029 AML and Mental Health Awareness Week posts — already 2026-05-17-evening / 2026-05-17), Ben Westwood (15-years-ago public speaker — already 2026-05-17), Dane Pedro (mental wellness leadership — already 2026-05-17), Sarah Logeswaran (Burges Salmon Consumer Duty H1 2026 — already 2026-05-15-evening), Heeral Gudka (All Reasonable Steps Briefing Paper — already 2026-05-14, surfaced again via Lorraine Rew reshare). Chantelle's AML Unfiltered launch is the standout because it's a substantive build, not a conference plug.
  • Rachel Clelland affiliation note: post signs off "At Self Space, we're helping firms put people over policy" — suggests she sits at or alongside Self Space (wellbeing / culture consultancy) rather than as a pure-finance Finance Director. Routing to ICP rather than Influencer because the post itself reads as practitioner conduct-risk framing aimed at finance & compliance peers, not a vendor pitch.

2026-05-17 (Sunday evening) — Sunday evening scan after the morning run. Re-walked all 13 watchlist profiles to catch anything posted later in the day or anything substantive that wasn't the strongest pick first time round: Chantelle Obatolu had a second qualifying original post (3d, UK digital ID 2029 rollout, with an AML / FinCrime read on what changes — KYC, onboarding, identity verification) that wasn't the one logged this morning, so adding now. Recommendation: next scheduled run lean harder on Audience C named-account scan (Lewis Silkin, Dentons, Hogan Lovells, CIPD, People Management, Personnel Today, Big 4) at the start of the keyword phase, so Track-2 comment-mining underneath their posts becomes the primary engine on weekends rather than the trailing tactic.
  • Path 5 active-buyer cluster: dry again, fifth run in a row producing nothing actionable in the UK ICP. The cluster appears genuinely sparse outside of weekday business hours.
  • Compliance Consultant UK card uses a people-search URL fallback per skill guidance (no confirmed slug for the company page activity surfaced in search).

2026-05-17 (Sunday) — Sunday morning scan. Watchlist scan executed first across all 13 named profiles: five qualifying posts surfaced (Adrianna Fabijanska FCA Working Together to Fight Financial Crime conference recap, Chantelle Obatolu Mental Health Awareness Week cultural-silence post, Emma Williams Stop asking so many questions opinion, Ben Westwood 15-years-ago you-won't-be-a-public-speaker reflection, Dane Pedro mental wellness & leadership post). Remaining 8 watchlist profiles were dry or only housekeeping (Aisling Twomey, Alasdair Fraser, Kostyantyn Mukhlygin, Samuel Aidoo, Scott Mitchell, Alan Campbell, Bibi Pearce Johnson, Miloš Bogdanović — mostly ICA Future of FinCrime Summit speaker promos & hiring posts). Watchlist routing: all 5 land at the top of today's section. Then ran ~14 past-week keyword searches across Paths 1 / 3 / 4 / 5 (Audience A), Audience B Track-1 (three lines of defence, FCA NFM, Consumer Duty outcomes, COCON, dignity at work), and Audience C influencer modifiers. Practitioner desert continues for the fourth Saturday-to-Sunday in a row: workplace investigation, ER/HR casework, difficult conversations, learning transfer, tick-box compliance, knowing-doing gap and AI roleplay clusters all returned either single-reaction practitioner posts (Charlie Nightingale, James Skinner, Laura MacDonald, Laura Maguire, Lisetta De Murtas) or vendor / consultancy noise (Compliance Consultant UK, Compliance Compass UK, 10xLEADER, Nu-Ology, JKE Fire & Security). Path 5 active-buyer searches produced nothing actionable for the fourth run in a row — the cluster is genuinely scarce on weekend mornings, flagging this pattern for the next review. NFM/COCON cluster produced Patrick Brodie (RPC Head of Employment Engagement & Equality, 38 reactions, 4d ago) with a strong practitioner-voiced observation that NFM is shifting from set-piece boardroom panel into live work conversations — lands the RTS thesis squarely on capability vs policy. Employment Rights Act cluster produced Will Chalk (Ashurst Partner, 25 reactions, 1d ago) framing the Act as the most fundamental change in UK employment in a generation. Dedup hits this run: Lisa Bell (Tell Jane zoom-bombing, logged 2026-05-14-pm) appeared again in Worker Protection cluster, Fiona McPhail (consultant, 1d ago, 0 visible reactions) fell below threshold so skipped. Next run priority: open the comment sections on Patrick Brodie's and Will Chalk's posts to Track-2 mine the practitioners commenting underneath — that's the most reliable way to backfill Audience A and B given the keyword-search drought.

2026-05-16 (Saturday) — Saturday morning scan. Ran ~22 past-week content searches across Paths 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, Audience B Track-1 clusters and Audience C influencer modifiers. Three candidates produced, all Audience C again. Audience A and B practitioner pickings remain thin: Worker Protection / Employment Rights Act 2025 clusters dominated by law-firm posts (Darwin Gray, Doyle Clayton, Ben Stepney, Nick Hobden) below the 25-reaction threshold; NFM cluster dominated by surveillance vendors (NICE Actimize, Morae, Doyle Clayton, NAVEX); three-lines-of-defence, Consumer Duty, EHRC enforcement, FCA Dear CEO and Acas grievance clusters near-empty for direct practitioner leads. Path 5 active-buyer search bank produced nothing actionable for the third run in a row — flagging this for review (the cluster may genuinely be rare on weekends). Path 3 AI roleplay search returned only Paradiso AI / 10xLEADER vendor self-promo. The strongest single pick is Eileen Donnelly — CEO/founder, 1st-degree connection, 81+ reactions on a deeply personal sexual-harassment-and-manager-capability post that lands the "corridor moment the law can't reach" argument almost word-for-word on RTS thesis. Lesley Vallance (101+ reactions, 6 comments, 7 reposts) and Amy McKeown (32 reactions, 10 comments) carry the psychosocial-risk / ISO 45003 frame — both are consultant voices but their commenters are practitioner-rich and worth Track-2 mining next run. Dedup hits this run: Heather Scales (untrained-managers post, logged 2026-05-15-evening), John Whitfield (learning transfer meta-analyses, logged 2026-05-15-evening), Sara Marshall (Employment Rights Act, logged 2026-05-14), Sarah Logeswaran (Burges Salmon, logged 2026-05-15-evening), Lisa Bell (Tell Jane zoom-bombing, logged 2026-05-14-pm). Next run should open the comment sections on Eileen Donnelly's post and Lesley Vallance's post and pull practitioner commenters to balance the audience mix — this should produce the Audience A/B leads the keyword searches are missing.

Influencer TODO

Run notes — 2026-05-16 (Saturday)

  • Three candidates total, all Audience C this run. Audience A and B practitioner pickings remained thin across NFM, Worker Protection / Employment Rights Act, Consumer Duty, three-lines-of-defence, EHRC, FCA Dear CEO, Acas grievance and Path 5 buyer-intent clusters — vendor and law-firm dominated.
  • Strongest pick: Eileen Donnelly (CEO/founder, 1st-degree connection, 81+ reactions) — deeply personal sexual harassment post that lands the "does the manager in front of me know how to hold this moment" argument almost word-for-word on RTS thesis. The 1st-degree relationship plus the personal nature of the post means the comment must be warmer and more careful than usual.
  • Lesley Vallance (101+ reactions, 7 reposts) is the largest stage of the run — psychosocial hazards reframed as leadership / operational / safety issues rather than HR. Slightly more promotional than ideal but the framing is dead on thesis.
  • Amy McKeown (32 reactions, 10 comments) is a smaller stage but a sharper consultant POV — she's pushing back on the "assessment first" reflex which is exactly the conversation RTS wants L&D buyers having.
  • Path 5 active-buyer search bank produced nothing actionable for the third run in a row — this is now a pattern worth surfacing to Toby. The cluster may genuinely be rare on weekends; recommend running the full Path 5 bank on Monday and reassessing.
  • Dedup hits: Heather Scales (untrained-managers, logged 2026-05-15-evening), John Whitfield (learning transfer meta-analyses, 2026-05-15-evening), Sara Marshall (Employment Rights Act, 2026-05-14), Sarah Logeswaran (Burges Salmon, 2026-05-15-evening), Lisa Bell (Tell Jane, 2026-05-14-pm).
  • Next-run priority: open the comment sections on Eileen Donnelly's post and Lesley Vallance's post — both should yield ICP-matching practitioner commenters that can be routed to Audience A (HR / L&D) or B (Compliance) to balance the mix.

2026-05-15 (Friday late) — fourth run of the day, lighter scan to fill in remaining audience coverage after the evening run. Ran ~15 past-week content searches across Paths 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, Audience B Track-1 clusters and Audience C influencer modifiers. Five candidates produced, all routed to Audience C again. Audience A and B practitioner pickings continue to be thin this week: NFM, Consumer Duty, EHRC, three-lines-of-defence, and Section 166 clusters were almost entirely vendor and law-firm dominated. Path 5 active-buyer cluster surfaced only generic recruiter / vendor copy (Kahoot, Synthesia adjacent), nothing actionable. The strongest single pick is Rujuta Singh on nice vs kind (97 reactions, 77 comments) — right on the difficult-conversations thesis. Hayley Lewis on the CMI 80% accidental managers number (76+ reactions) gives a clean Path 2 stage. Alan Ward (Stephenson Harwood) on FCA NFM and leadership-conduct whistleblowing (58 reactions) is the cleanest Audience B-adjacent stage of the run. Daz Cox (Calico) launching Voices in the Dark VR training is right in the rehearsal-not-policy lane — useful peer rather than competitor framing. Felicity Menzies (Culture Plus, Australia) is more advertorial than the rest but threshold-passing at 25 reactions and 9 reposts and sits squarely on RTS thesis (psychosocial safety, trauma-informed leadership, Australian regulated risk). One Oyin Sotoyinbo (LendInvest, Head of Risk & Compliance) post on the ICA Summit Blueprint to Behaviour session hit dedup — he last logged 2026-05-11. Next run should weight harder on Audience B Track-2 comment-mining (open Alan Ward's NFM post and any large Worker Protection law-firm post and pull practitioner commenters) to balance the audience mix.

Run notes — 2026-05-15 (late)

  • Five candidates total, all Audience C this run. Audience A and B remained thin across NFM, Consumer Duty, three-lines-of-defence, Section 166, EHRC and Path 5 buyer-intent clusters — vendor and law-firm dominated.
  • Strongest pick: Rujuta Singh's nice-vs-kind post (97 reactions, 77 comments) — right on the difficult-conversations thesis and she's a 1st-degree connection.
  • Hayley Lewis's accidental manager podcast launch is the cleanest Path 2 stage of the run.
  • Alan Ward (Stephenson Harwood) gives the cleanest Audience B-adjacent stage on FCA NFM — recommend mining his comment section in the next run.
  • Daz Cox's Voices in the Dark is genuine peer territory for RTS — immersive practice for harassment response in the night-time economy.
  • Felicity Menzies passes the 25-reaction floor but it's an advertorial "buy our programme" post — included because the framing (psychosocial safety as regulated risk) is dead on thesis. Comment is intentionally a little more skeptical / question-led.
  • Dedup: Oyin Sotoyinbo (LendInvest, ICA Summit Blueprint to Behaviour) skipped — logged 2026-05-11.
  • Path 5 active-buyer search bank produced nothing actionable again — the cluster is rare even on a full scan; flag if it stays dry for one more run.

2026-05-15 (Friday evening) — third run of the day, first successful one. Browser pairing restored, ran ~14 past-week content searches across Paths 1, 2, 4 and Audience B/C clusters plus Audience C influencer modifiers. Six candidates produced, all routed to Audience C this run. Direct Audience A and B practitioner pickings were notably thin: NFM / Worker Protection / Consumer Duty clusters were vendor and law-firm dominated, the L&D and conduct-risk practitioner searches surfaced almost entirely thought-leadership posts and one Oyin Sotoyinbo (LendInvest) post that hit dedup (he already logged 2026-05-11). Strongest single pick: John Whitfield on learning transfer citing Nimante et al. 2025 at 109+ reactions — right on Real Talk Studio's thesis. Heather Scales (UK FCIPD consultant) on untrained managers at 95+ reactions and Lisa Drake's Channel 4 mediator CPD at 90+ are the other two big stages. Will Taylor (Compassa H&S) translates the same tick-box critique into Worker Protection territory cleanly. Sarah Logeswaran (Burges Salmon) and Keith Black (US workplace investigator) are smaller but on-thesis. Path 5 active-buyer search bank produced nothing actionable — the L&D / e-learning recommendation cluster surfaced spam (SAP / generic certificate posts) and the manager-training recommendation cluster surfaced US-coaching-recruiter content. Logged here as a normal run despite the Audience A/B thinness; next run should weight Audience B Track-2 comment-mining harder (open the bigger law-firm Worker Protection posts and pull the practitioner commenters) to balance the mix.

1. Oksana Lukash

CHRO / Audience A (4x Chief People Officer running the Gen Z retention / onboarding redesign frame with an explicit manager-training-before-headcount line that lands exactly on the Real Talk thesis on first-time / new-manager readiness)
CHRO TODO
Role
4x Chief People Officer (CPO) · People Strategy & Organizational Transformation · 7x M&A · 5 CEO Transitions · ESG · Board & Executive Advisor · TEDx Speaker · Best-Selling Author · senior practitioner with sign-off authority · hires across biotech (iMDx, Avid, Avanir) so the operating environment is regulated and high-stakes · 2nd-degree connection
Post topic
Argument that Gen Z is voting with their feet faster than any prior generation and the relationship is built or broken in the first 6–12 months. Five additions to her onboarding: pay range transparency, manager training before headcount, mental health day on day one, co-written 30/60/90 plan, clear answer to where can I be in 18 months. Explicit line: if the manager is not ready, the new hire will not stay long enough for it to matter. Closes with the diagnostic question: if you are losing Gen Z talent at the 6–12 month mark, look at your onboarding before you look at your engagement.
Engagement
1 reaction · 0 comments · very fresh post (~2h old) so engagement is still building · below the typical reaction threshold but the post sits exactly on the Real Talk thesis on manager-readiness and Oksana is a serious 4x CPO buyer-voice, so the value of being early to the comment thread outweighs the soft reaction count

~2h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Yeah, this is good. The manager training before headcount line is the bit that really matters, the relationship gets built or broken in those first weeks and most onboarding plans assume the manager is already ready. How are you testing the manager is ready?
Connection request
Hi Oksana, really useful post on the Gen Z onboarding changes. The manager training before headcount line stood out. Would love to swap notes on how you're testing whether managers are actually ready before new hires land.

Post topic
Conduct appears long before misconduct is reported — the conditions form much earlier, in hesitation, in silence, in meetings where challenge gradually disappears, in decisions people comply with while privately carrying concern. Culture is not only expressed through values statements; it becomes visible through what people feel safe raising, questioning and escalating in practice. Tagged with #SMCR and #BoardGovernance. Almost word-for-word the Real Talk thesis on early conduct signals being behavioural rather than reportable.

Post topic
Five takeaways from a California scheduling masterclass (300+ California employers, HR professionals, attorneys registered). Item 5: manager training is the cheapest reasonable step — a documented 30-minute training, one a month, is one of the strongest pieces of evidence under the 2024 PAGA reform. Bottom line: the gap between a manageable wage and hour dispute and a seven-figure PAGA case is usually built shift by shift, long before anyone files anything. Reasonable-steps-as-documented-evidence frame sits exactly on the Real Talk thesis on what evidence holds up under enforcement.
Engagement
0 visible reactions · 0 comments · ~3h old · very fresh post and Anthony's audience is US employment lawyers / HR — soft engagement but the all-reasonable-steps frame translates to UK so the comment travels well to a UK audience reading his thread · below the standard Audience C threshold but kept in because the framing is uncommonly clean on the reasonable-steps-as-evidence point

1. Lisa Bell

Founder of Tell Jane · Audience C (UK harassment / misconduct / culture-risk consultancy founder running the Zoom-bombing-during-her-own-ER-webinar story straight into a Worker Protection Act October 2026 third-party / online harassment frame — matured from AM near-miss to live pick on a 10x jump in comments)
Influencer TODO
Role
Founder of Tell Jane · helps organisations prevent and respond to workplace sexual harassment, misconduct and culture risks · 2nd-degree connection · was running the Smarter ER: Building Systems That Stop Issues Before They Start webinar series when the incident happened, so the post sits naturally on top of her recurring practitioner cadence rather than as a one-off · flagged as a near-miss in the AM run at 3 reactions, 19h old, has since matured to 31 comments which makes it the highest-engagement Audience C item of the day
Post topic
First-person story of being Zoom-bombed mid-webinar by an uninvited guest who shouted an obscenity and drew a penis on her screen for a virtual room of HR people. Uses it as a hook into the bigger employer point: Worker Protection Act covers online harassment too, October 2026 levels-up the all-reasonable-steps bar and extends liability to third-party harassment, and most harassment policies have a gap because they don't explicitly cover online harassment / company webinars / virtual events. Direct rhetorical ask: if this happened in one of your company webinars, would you know what to do?
Engagement
3 reactions · 31 comments · 20h old · comment count well above the typical Audience C threshold and well above the morning's high-watermark on the all-reasonable-steps cluster · live thread, replies still landing · strong stage for a fast comment, comment section is also worth a Track-2 mining pass for HR practitioners who have stepped in with similar incidents

20h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment

Post topic
Book contribution announcement that pivots into the bigger transformation pattern she's seeing: businesses asking how should we use AI when the better question is what challenges are we facing that AI might actually help with. Walks through three live use cases with named pain (best people leaving, slow workforce planning, inconsistent performance conversations across the business). On the third one, the explicit line is that AI can help managers prepare in ways that actually land — exact Real Talk territory on the manager-prep-before-the-conversation side. Closes with start with the problem, the tool follows.
Engagement
5 reactions · 1 comment · under the typical Audience C floor on reaction count but the line on inconsistent performance conversations sits exactly on the Real Talk thesis and Kate is a high-quality MCIPD transformation voice worth showing up next to · book-launch posts also tend to mature over 48–72h on her audience · early reply move
Post age
~5m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Really useful framing. The inconsistent performance conversations bit is the one most managers don't trip on in prep, they trip on the first sentence under pressure. How are you seeing them solve this?
Connection request
Hi Kate, really enjoyed your post on AI for People Professionals. Would love to swap notes on how you're seeing managers actually close the gap between AI-assisted prep and what lands in the moment.

3. Liz Howard (CPsychol)

Chartered Coaching Psychologist · OD Consultant at Up2eleven · Audience C (UK psychology-led leadership development consultant explicitly running the training-alone-doesn't-change-behaviour line and a blended classroom + psychometrics + coaching prescription — sits exactly on the Real Talk frame on the awareness vs application gap)
Influencer TODO
Role
Chartered Psychologist · Executive Coach · OD Consultant · 20+ years across leadership, talent and OD · works with large complex organisations on emotional intelligence, performance and leading in complexity · runs Up2eleven Leadership Coaching & OD Consulting · 2nd-degree connection · positions explicitly as the EI / behaviour-change practitioner not a generic leadership consultant
Post topic
Argument that we're asking more of leaders than ever, EQ is now core capability not a nice-to-have, and yet EQ is still developed through training alone — which builds some awareness, but awareness alone doesn't change behaviour. Names the shift in the people agenda as moving from learning about leadership to embedding leadership capability in practice. Lists what EQ development actually requires: reflection in real situations, feedback that actually lands, the ability to notice patterns and adapt, ongoing support to embed change. Concludes blended approaches (classroom + psychometrics + coaching) accelerate development. Exact Real Talk thesis on the awareness-vs-application gap.
Engagement
2 reactions · 0 comments · ~11m old · below the typical Audience C reaction threshold but the post is a near-perfect ICP-language match (training alone doesn't change behaviour / awareness alone doesn't shift it / blended approaches) and Liz is a high-quality Chartered Psychologist voice with 20+ years in OD · early reply window, low cost

~11m

4. Cass Coulston

Managing Director, Beacon Consulting · Audience C (UK leadership consulting MD announcing a peer-reviewed IJ paper co-authored with King's College London Institute of Psychiatry, funded by Deloitte Human Capital · sits in the leader-tension / signalled-through-behaviour territory next to the Real Talk thesis on what actually lands)
Influencer TODO
Role
Managing Director, Beacon Consulting · Helps leaders and teams think bigger, connect deeper and lead with impact · 2nd-degree connection · just published in the International Journal of Qualitative Studies on Health and Well-being on adaptive leadership in virtual / hybrid work · co-authored with King's College London Institute of Psychiatry, Psychology & Neuroscience (Myanna Duncan, Sukhi Shergill, Ricardo Twumasi) and supported by independent funding from Deloitte Human Capital · high-credibility academic-industry bridge profile
Post topic
Announcement of the new IJQSHW paper How do we want to be led? Three themes from interviews and focus groups across leaders and team members globally: (1) Connected Leadership Aspirations — people want meaningful connection not more communication, especially in dispersed work; (2) Leadership as Positive Social Influence — trust, autonomy, psychological safety, well-being and healthy boundaries are signalled through behaviour, not communicated through words; (3) The Leadership Tension — the behaviours that help teams (connection, care, trust, availability, emotional presence) have become increasingly demanding for leaders when not properly resourced. Ends on the design question: how do we make this kind of leadership sustainable.
Engagement
0 reactions visible at first capture · very fresh window · research-paper launch posts typically draw senior HR / OD / academic reactions over the first 48h · below the typical Audience C threshold but the King's / Deloitte / IJQSHW credibility stack makes this a high-quality stage to be on the front page of
Post age
~now
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Sharp read. The leadership tension bit is the one most programmes skip, you can teach the connection behaviours fine, the hard bit is making them sustainable when leaders are already stretched. How are you designing for that?
Connection request
Hi Cass, really useful post on the connected leadership research. Would love to swap notes on the leadership tension bit, particularly how teams design for sustainable connection rather than just adding more to the leader's plate.

Run notes — 2026-05-14 PM

  • Six candidates this run, all Audience C. No clean Audience A practitioner direct hit and no clean Audience B FS practitioner this afternoon. Pattern consistent with the AM run and the broader two-week thread — UK CHRO / Head of L&D / Head of Compliance practitioners are not posting on past-week + sorted-by-date in volumes that clear the bar this week. Switching the sort to most-relevant on a subsequent run is the obvious adjustment to test on Friday.
  • Strongest pick: Lisa Bell (Tell Jane). Promoted from this morning's near-miss list. AM run captured her at 3 reactions, 19h old; the post has since matured to 31 comments, by far the highest Audience C engagement of the day. The Zoom-bombing-during-her-own-ER-webinar story is a sharp narrative hook into the Worker Protection Act October 2026 third-party / online harassment frame and the post's comment section is alive enough to be worth a Track-2 mining pass on Friday for HR practitioners who have stepped in with similar incidents.
  • Audience C standout on Real Talk-language match: Kate Maddison-Greenwell. The specific line in her post — AI can help managers prepare in ways that actually land — followed immediately by the inconsistent performance conversations across the business pain point, is as close as Audience C language gets to the Real Talk thesis without naming a competitor. Worth treating as a long-term influencer relationship, not just a one-off reply. Liz Howard's awareness alone doesn't change behaviour and Cass Coulston's signalled through behaviour, not communicated through words sit on the same axis.
  • Cass Coulston is a structurally high-value relationship even at zero reactions. Beacon Consulting MD + King's College London Institute of Psychiatry co-authorship + Deloitte Human Capital independent funding + freshly published IJQSHW peer-reviewed paper. Replying early to a research-launch post on connected leadership puts a comment from Toby in front of the Deloitte / King's / IJQSHW academic-industry audience that typically converges on the comments over the first 48h. Worth re-checking the post on Saturday for the maturity-curve mining pass.
  • Path 5 (active-buyer) bucket dry again. Any-recommendations / looking-for / best-platform searches all came back off-target (Indian e-learning vendor noise, GCC career posts, FDA / Bank-of-Tanzania content). Path 5 has now been dry for three consecutive runs — worth flagging as a pattern. Hypothesis: the question-format heuristics (118–121) are noisy and the past-week filter is throwing them off; on Friday try the competitor-mention searches (114–117) which are higher-precision.
  • Audience A practitioner bucket empty for the third run in a row. Path 1 (workplace investigation, ER casework, EHRC / Worker Protection / SMCR / COCON / NFM) and Path 2 (knowing-doing gap, difficult conversations, first-time manager, manager development, learning transfer / training transfer / didn't stick) searches all surfaced lots of off-ICP content (Indian recruitment, Pakistani law, French coaches, Australian leadership, US K-12 education) and a handful of Audience C consultants but no UK Head of L&D / Head of ER / Head of Manager Development / CHRO voice clearing the bar.
  • Audience B FS practitioner bucket also empty. Three lines of defence search threw Donald Davies (consultant, not practitioner) and Yannick Bigot (MENA fintech COO — geo wrong, already flagged in AM run). FCA / Consumer Duty / Head of Compliance searches mostly returned Tanzanian banking jobs, French / Italian content, ACAMS-France-board content (Baptiste Forestier — Head of Compliance at Flowdesk, on the right frame but France-based and engagement at 0). No UK FCA practitioner clearing the bar.
  • Competitive-watch signal: Cecil L. Ivey, Sr. · CEO of GetOpossum.com · running an AI voice training simulations pitch on the rehearse-the-crisis-before-the-phone-rings frame for contact centres & organ procurement. This is closer to Real Talk's territory than Catalyst AI (yesterday's watch) — specifically the rehearsal-space / pressure-controlled-gym / wire-better-habits-before-the-red-light-blinks language. Different vertical (contact centres / insurance / organ procurement) so not a direct competitor for the L&D / compliance buyer, but worth tracking the framing. Awarathon (Pharma sales coaching) also surfaced on the same search — vertical-specific.
  • Near-misses flagged: Biebele K. (Culture & HR Consultant) — fear-vs-trust management post, 3 comments, on-topic but the post leans observation rather than question, parking for a re-look if she posts again; Donald Davies (Cybernetics author / GRC consultant) — SMCR / conduct risk visibility-vs-volume line, sharp framing but 0 engagement and post leans heavily into a DM-me-for-GRC-as-a-Service close which makes the comment section less useful as a stage; Tibor Dudjik (Fractional COO) — founder-trust-with-AI piece, interesting angle but tangent to ICP; The Soltesz Institute (payments / banking governance Fourth-Line-of-Defence essay) — right frame, wrong audience (payments / banking practitioner not L&D / HR); Yannick Bigot — MENA fintech again, same geo skip as AM.
  • Search bank coverage this run: Path 1 (Worker Protection Act + all reasonable steps, NFM, EHRC / Worker Protection harassment, preventive duty + third-party harassment, grievance / workplace investigation HR, employee relations casework, tribunal harassment / discrimination, SMCR / COCON), Path 2 (difficult conversations manager, first-time manager / new manager, performance conversation / underperformance manager, manager effectiveness / manager capability, learning transfer / training transfer / didn't stick L&D, leadership development behaviour change, speak-up culture / psychological safety, knowing-doing gap), Path 3 (AI roleplay / conversation simulation), Path 4 (mandatory training doesn't work / boring / pointless, compliance theatre / tick box), Path 5 (any-recommendations training / e-learning / LMS), Audience B (three lines of defence compliance, Consumer Duty + vulnerable, Consumer Duty + year 2 / outcomes, FCA conduct / culture / Dear CEO, Head of Compliance / Chief Compliance FCA), Audience C (Lewis Silkin / Pinsent Masons / Bates Wells + harassment / misconduct). 28 searches in total this run, roughly on the Friday rotation target.
  • Path adjustment for Friday: raise Path 3 sample (AI roleplay / conversation simulation / behavioural simulation / immersive learning / VR training) since this week's competitor-watch surface (Cecil Ivey, Catalyst AI, Awarathon, Pridmora) has been the densest signal cluster; lower Path 5 question-format heuristics and lean on competitor-mention searches instead.
  • Default link format: people-search URLs as per skill instruction.

2026-05-14 (Thursday AM) — six-candidate run weighted heavily toward Audience C: a strong Audience B FS practitioner (Cole Ripley Payne running the Provision 29 + NFM frame in the same post with the without-testing-controls-are-just-hopes line) plus five Audience C consultants and lawyers running ICP-shaped framings on the Worker Protection Act / reasonable steps shift, first-time manager development, and how workplace investigation confidentiality actually feels for the complainant. No clean Path 5 active-buyer and no direct CHRO/HR practitioner pick this morning; engagement counts are low across the board (past-week filter, very fresh posts). Run notes below flag near-misses, dry searches, and the Audience C reaction-threshold trade-off.

1. Cole Ripley Payne

Risk Leader focused on developing people-centred culture · Audience B (UK FS risk practitioner running the Provision 29 controls shift and the FCA NFM culture-as-risk-and-control frame in the same post — explicit without-testing-controls-are-just-hopes line)
ICP TODO
Role
Risk Leader positioning explicitly around developing people-centred culture · 2nd-degree connection · UK FS practitioner voice · posts under the "What's the tea in the world of risk, governance and controls?" running newsletter format so this is a recurring practitioner cadence not a one-off · same post surfaced under both NFM/FCA and three lines of defence keyword searches this morning, which is the kind of cross-search hit pattern that flags a strong practitioner
Post topic
Tight, sharp practitioner post covering two FS regulatory moves in one sweep. First the move from historical financial controls to wider key/material controls for FY26/27 (Living Provision 29), with the line that without testing, controls are just hopes — and that reporting needs a strong narrative beyond just R/A/G dashboards. Second the FCA NFM piece, where extending accountability of behaviour beyond financial tasks suddenly makes culture less about "everyone in the office under the guise of togetherness" and more about the behaviours people actually show. Exact Real Talk frame on culture-as-control where the control is a manager handling a conversation, not a documented process.
Engagement
0–1 reactions visible · ~1m old at first capture, still in the very-early window · below the typical Audience B engagement floor but the practitioner-language cross-search hit and the unusual double-framing (Provision 29 + NFM in the same post) make this a high-quality early-window pick · worth a low-cost early reply
Post age
~1m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Sharp read. Without testing, controls are just hopes lands hardest on the NFM side, because the control is a manager handling a real conversation, not a documented process. How are you seeing teams actually test it?
Connection request
Hi Cole, sharp read on the Provision 29 and NFM piece in one sweep. Would love to swap notes on how you're seeing teams actually test the culture-as-control bit, especially where the control is a manager handling a real conversation.

Workplace harassment / EDI consultant · writer of The Unity Playbook · Audience C (UK consultant releasing an All Reasonable Steps briefing paper that maps the documented-current-owned evidence bar for the Worker Protection preventative duty)
Influencer TODO
Role
Helps HR teams and leadership navigate sexual harassment duties, competing rights, and religion/belief conflicts · writer of The Unity Playbook and Freedom of Expression / Belief Conflicts as the new EDI frontier · 2nd-degree connection · positions explicitly as the EDI / harassment-duty bridge between HR and legal
Post topic
Launch of an All Reasonable Steps briefing paper as of May 2026. Opens with a scenario — tomorrow morning your phone rings, a senior manager has been found to have sexually harassed a colleague, plus a separate discrimination claim alleging that an objection to facilities policy was treated as misconduct. The framing question: can you evidence reasonable steps that are documented, current, and owned? Or can you only point to the policies that say you would? Briefing maps the Worker Protection preventative duty, whistleblowing protections and the wider EDI risk stack. Lands the documented-current-owned bar that most policies fail on because owned means someone actually rehearsed the response.
Engagement
8 reactions · 23h old, edited · below the 25-reaction Audience C floor but on a niche practitioner topic where 8 from a 2nd-degree EDI consultant is a credible signal · the briefing-paper format itself is the kind of asset that draws HR/legal practitioner comments worth Track-2 mining on the next pass

~23h

Find on LinkedIn

Really useful framing. The documented, current, owned line is the bit most policies fall down on, because owned means someone actually rehearsed the response, not just signed off the doc. How are you seeing HR teams prove that?

3. Chloe Wallace

Trauma-informed sexual harassment specialist · Yorkshire HR consultant · Audience C (UK harassment / misconduct trainer with a sharp MHAW post on how managers respond to sensitive disclosures in the seconds after they land)
Influencer TODO
Role
Assoc CIPD · positions as the most unique workplace sexual harassment and misconduct prevention training in the UK · trauma-informed specialist · award-winning generalist HR support for Yorkshire businesses · 2nd-degree connection · sits in the harassment-prevention training space directly adjacent to Real Talk's pitch
Post topic
Mental Health Awareness Week framing piece on sensitive disclosures at work. Lists the kinds of disclosures landing in workplaces every day — domestic abuse, substance misuse, debt, miscarriage, fertility treatment, terminal diagnosis, bereavement, self-harm, sexual assault, homelessness — and reframes them as not difficult conversations but really sensitive disclosures. The line that sticks: the person receiving the disclosure often has seconds to formulate a response, and that response shapes whether someone feels believed, safe, ashamed, supported, judged, dismissed or exposed. Exact Real Talk pitch on manager rehearsal of the high-stakes moment that lands in real time.
Engagement
1 reaction · 3m old at first capture, very early window · below the standard Audience C 25-reaction floor but the MHAW timing and the seconds-to-formulate-a-response frame are exactly on Real Talk's wavelength · worth an early reply for the practitioner relationship rather than the engagement

4. Kasia Murphy

Business Psychologist · Leadership Coach for first-time managers · Audience C (UK coach landing the need-to-be-liked trap that keeps new managers from delivering hard feedback — classic first-time-manager readiness frame)

Role
Business Psychologist · Leadership Coach helping first-time managers become leaders people choose to follow · runs coaching, training and online courses · MSc Business Psychology · 2nd-degree connection · sits squarely in the first-time-manager development space that Real Talk targets
Post topic
Personal-story post: when she first had a team she tried so hard to be liked that she avoided the hard feedback, said yes when she meant no, and over-explained decisions hoping for approval. Frames it as one of the most common traps first-time managers fall into. The real cost is the uncomfortable conversations that never happen. Closes on being respected over being liked. Direct rehearsal-of-the-harder-conversation pitch from a coach voice, MHAW-week-adjacent, and the kind of first-time-manager content that draws L&D practitioner reactions.
Engagement
0–1 reactions visible · 15m old, very early window · below the standard Audience C 25-reaction floor but the post is well-formed and on-topic for the L&D buying path · including as an early-reply rather than a high-engagement stage

Post topic
Newsletter post on the Employment Rights Act 2025 changes to workplace harassment law. Lists the three shifts SMEs need to know: new responsibilities around third-party harassment, stronger protections for whistleblowers, and the higher duty to take all reasonable steps to prevent harassment. Closes on practical steps SMEs can start taking now around training, policies, reporting procedures and workplace culture. Adjacent to Heeral Gudka and Sara Thomas content already in the tracker but written for a different (SME, plain-English) audience.

6. Xiao Pan

UK Employment Expert · Law Gazette Power Player · Audience C (employment-rights voice running an unusually well-observed piece on what workplace investigation confidentiality feels like for the person who raised the issue — the carry-on-as-normal bit)
Influencer TODO
Role
Employment Expert · Law Gazette Power Player · former recruiter / sales specialist / polyglot · 2nd-degree connection · sits at the employment-rights / NDA / investigation reform end of the influencer pool
Post topic
Personal-voice essay reframing the workplace investigation experience from the complainant's side. Opens on NDAs as the final nail in the coffin, then pivots: even without an NDA you still feel silenced, because the moment a workplace investigation starts everything becomes private and confidential. Acknowledges the legitimate reasons for confidentiality (fairness, witness protection, evidence integrity) but lands the line that the complainant is expected to carry on as normal while something deeply upsetting is examined behind closed doors, replaying conversations while still showing up to work meetings. Exact Real Talk pitch on manager handling of the colleague during a live investigation — the part most ER training skips because it sits on behaviour, not procedure.
Engagement
0–1 reactions visible · 50m old, early window · below the standard Audience C 25-reaction floor · Law Gazette Power Player flag and the unusually well-observed framing makes this a quality stage despite the low count · the carry-on-as-normal frame is the kind of line that can be borrowed and built on in Toby's voice without competing
Post age
~50m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Really useful framing. The carry-on-as-normal bit is the part most investigation training skips, because it sits on how the manager handles the colleague mid-process, not on the procedure. What have you seen actually help?
Connection request
Hi Xiao, really useful post on what investigation confidentiality feels like from the complainant's side. Would love to swap notes on what you've seen actually help managers handle the carry-on-as-normal bit when the case is still live.

Run notes — 2026-05-14 AM

  • Six candidates this run. One Audience B (Cole Ripley Payne, fresh window, ~1m old) and five Audience C consultants / employment lawyers. Audience C reaction counts are noticeably low across the past-week filter; only Heeral Gudka clears 5 reactions (8 reactions, 23h old). Including the rest as early-reply moves rather than high-engagement stages.
  • Strongest pick: Cole Ripley Payne (Audience B). UK FS risk practitioner running the Provision 29 controls shift and FCA NFM culture-as-risk-and-control frame in the same post, with the without-testing-controls-are-just-hopes line. Surfaced under two different searches this morning (the NFM/FCA cluster and the three-lines-of-defence cluster) which is the kind of cross-search hit that flags a quality practitioner. ~1m old at first capture — very early window, worth replying before the post matures.
  • Audience C standout: Heeral Gudka. All Reasonable Steps Briefing Paper launch post, 8 reactions, 23h old. Below the 25-reaction floor but the briefing-paper format itself draws HR/legal practitioner reactions and is worth Track-2 mining once the post matures.
  • Audience C honourable mentions: Chloe Wallace (MHAW + sensitive disclosures), Kasia Murphy (first-time manager need-to-be-liked trap), Sara Marshall (SME Employment Rights Act 2025 newsletter), Xiao Pan (workplace investigation confidentiality from the complainant's side). All 0–1 reactions but on-topic framings worth replying to early. Sara Marshall is a deliberate broader-audience pick (SME owners and HR managers rather than FS / 500-plus tier).
  • CHRO bucket empty for the second run in a row. Path 5 active-buyer searches (any-recommendations training/e-learning, looking-for platform / leadership programme) all came back off-target (Pakistani / Indian / French / Indonesian content, irrelevant hiring posts, Quran-quote spam, generic recruitment threads). Audience A Path 1 (workplace investigation lessons, employee relations / ER casework HR, reasonable-steps harassment employer) and Path 2 (knowing-doing gap, manager development / leadership development programme, CIPD manager training, Head of L&D / Head of Learning) keyword searches surfaced lots of off-ICP content and a handful of useful Audience C posts but no direct CHRO/HR practitioner voice clearing the bar.
  • Near-misses flagged: Lisa Bell (Founder, Tell Jane) — Zoom-bombed mid-Smarter ER webinar post, 3 reactions, 19h old, very fresh story angle but the post is heavily focused on the platform safety incident rather than the ER content itself, parking for now and watching the follow-up; Theophilus Olanrewaju (Trainee Solicitor, Herrington Carmichael) — sharp post on the October 2026 all reasonable steps shift but trainee-level so not the influencer tier; Sara Thomas / Pinsent (already in tracker); Cole's same post on FCA NFM hit twice; Mohamed Tayel (Senior AML Compliance Officer, EU-based, 7th-century-market-regulation philosophical piece — rich content but EU/MENA audience not UK ICP); Yannick Bigot (MENA fintech governance — geo wrong); Catalyst AI (productivity app vendor essay on the knowing-doing gap — competitor-adjacent, log in competitive watch).

Rotation continues on Friday.
  • Default link format: people-search URLs as per skill instruction. Post permalinks are not reliably extractable from inside the automation for non-owner viewers.

2026-05-12 (Tuesday AM, Mental Health Awareness Week continues) — lean four-candidate run: an Audience C headline pick from The TCM Group (Deb D., 39 reactions, learning-vs-development tick-box reframe), an Audience B early-window practitioner on CDD-as-theatre, plus two fresh Audience C consultants — one on FCA NFM behavioural readiness, one on MHAW manager training. No CHRO direct hits this run; Path 5 dry again on the morning sweep.

1. Marco B.

CAMS Financial Crime Specialist · FinCrime Agent Founder · Audience B (UK/EU AML practitioner contrasting CDD-as-checkbox with CDD-as-risk-assurance — compliance theatre framing)
ICP TODO
Role
CAMS Financial Crime Specialist with deep RegTech, sanctions, KYC/CDD and fraud-prevention background · founder/curator of FinCrime Agent · practitioner-style poster sitting at the intersection of AML compliance and AI tooling · small but engaged AML practitioner network
Post topic
Short, sharp side-by-side framing of two ways to run CDD: as a checkbox (collect documents, fill form, move on) versus as risk assurance (understand the customer, assess whether stated activity makes sense, challenge the front office when something does not add up, document reasoning rather than just findings). Closes with the line that the difference between the two approaches is the difference between compliance theatre and actual risk management — exact Real Talk Studio frame applied to AML casework.
Engagement
3 reactions · 47m old, still climbing · below the typical Audience B engagement floor but the practitioner-language framing and the explicit compliance-theatre vs risk-assurance distinction make this a strong early-window pick · same post reposted under his FinCrime Agent brand page
Post age
~47m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Really useful framing. The reasoning vs findings distinction is where most assurance work quietly drifts back into theatre, you can document the form and still miss what's actually off. How are you seeing teams test for the second version?
Connection request
Hi Marco, really useful breakdown of CDD as checkbox vs risk assurance. Would love to swap notes on how you're seeing teams build the muscle to challenge the front office when something doesn't quite add up.

2. Deb D.

Executive Leadership Coach at The TCM Group · UN Women UK Delegate · Audience C (39-reaction stage from a UK people-consultancy senior figure reframing L&D away from tick-box and toward embedding into everyday leadership practice — exact Real Talk frame)
Influencer TODO
Role
Executive Leadership & High Performance Coach at The TCM Group (UK conflict-management and people-transformation consultancy with a long-standing CIPD-adjacent reputation) · LTEM specialist · Certified Psychometrics Practitioner · UN Women UK Delegate · positions herself explicitly as a coach helping good leaders become great leaders and changemakers
Post topic
Reflection from a People Leader magazine interview: L&D should never be a one-off event, and future-focused organisations are the ones treating learning as part of the culture itself. The key reframe lands on the distinction between learning (expanding thinking, curiosity, insight) and development (embedding that growth into behaviours, leadership, decision-making, everyday practice). Explicit critique of training as a tick-box exercise — the exact frame Real Talk pitches against. TCM Group brand attached at the bottom; the post itself is a thought-leadership cut, not a sell.
Engagement
39 reactions · 1 comment · 3d old · clears the Audience C 25-reaction floor comfortably · reactions include Danny Wareham (UK L&D thought leader) so the comment section is the kind of L&D practitioner audience worth a Track-2 pass on the next run · strongest pick of the run
Post age
~3d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Spot on. The learning vs development distinction is what most programmes quietly skip, the curiosity bit lands fine in the workshop, the embedding bit is where it falls over. How are you seeing leaders make that shift stick?
Connection request
Hi Deb, really enjoyed your People Leader piece on moving beyond training as a tick-box. Would love to swap notes on what's worked when you're trying to embed development into everyday leadership behaviour rather than just expanding thinking.

3. Alex Austin

Sales Leader / Podcast Host · Helping businesses put people at the heart of strategy · Audience C (UK people-strategy consultant landing the exact Real Talk frame on the September 2026 NFM rules: behaviourally ready, not just policy ready)
Influencer TODO
Role
Sales Leader and Podcast Host positioning as a people-and-strategy consultant helping businesses succeed by putting people at the heart of strategy · 2nd-degree connection · routes to Audience C on poster role even though the framing is aimed squarely at HR / CHRO buyers
Post topic
Tight, direct post on the FCA's upcoming non-financial misconduct (NFM) changes that lands the exact Real Talk thesis from a different voice. Frames the regulator as pushing firms to be behaviourally ready not just policy ready, and lists the four questions that need answering: can your people speak up safely, can managers handle the big conversations, can your leaders challenge poor conduct consistently, do your employees trust what happens next? Closes with the point that the firms getting ahead are treating this as more than a compliance exercise and looking at leadership behaviour, culture and conditions. Could have been written from Real Talk's pitch deck.
Engagement
0–1 reactions visible · 16m old, very early window · below standard Audience C engagement floor but the framing is exact-fit and the post is precisely the kind of stage where a peer comment in Toby's voice adds genuine value rather than competes · worth a low-cost early reply
Post age
~16m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Sharp read. The behaviourally ready vs policy ready bit is exactly where most NFM programmes break, the policy lands fine, the first time a manager has to actually call out a senior leader is where it wobbles. What's working when you see firms close that gap?
Connection request
Hi Alex, really useful post on behavioural readiness for the NFM rules. Would love to swap notes on what you're seeing actually shift the manager-challenging-poor-conduct piece, not just the policy bit.

4. Asha Berzon

Founder, Mindbility · 17 years inside UK probation / high-pressure public sector · Audience C (UK consultant landing a sharp MHAW critique of training-as-wallpaper with explicit call for proper manager training on the harder 1-2-1)
Influencer TODO
Role
Founder of Mindbility · helps SMEs and public-sector organisations reduce burnout and build resilient teams · CPD-accredited, DISC, Five Behaviours-certified, 3x award winner · 2nd-degree connection · 17 years inside UK probation gives the post unusual public-sector credibility on the manager training question
Post topic
Mental Health Awareness Week framing piece: MHAW is the one week of the year it's safe to talk about mental health, which tells you everything about the other 51. Most managers were never trained for the emotional weight of the role they've been handed, and how they run a 1-2-1 directly affects whether someone sleeps that night. Cites the CIPD data point that workload and management style are the two biggest causes of UK work-related stress, and the Deloitte ROI of £5 per £1 spent on effective mental health provision. Closes with the call to ask line managers when they last had proper, practical training on psychological safety — not just a 45-minute e-learning module. Exact Real Talk pitch on manager rehearsal of the harder conversation.
Engagement
1 reaction · 17m old, very early window · below the standard Audience C 25-reaction floor but the post is unusually well-argued and MHAW-timely · surfaced twice this morning via two different searches (difficult conversations manager, and mental health manager training week) which suggests the piece is positioned to climb
Post age
~17m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Yeah, this is good. The bit about a 1-2-1 deciding whether someone sleeps that night is the line most senior leaders need to hear, the e-learning module never gets near that. How are you helping managers practise the harder ones?
Connection request
Hi Asha, really useful post on MHAW and the manager training gap. Would love to swap notes on what's worked when you're trying to get past the 45-minute e-learning and into the 1-2-1 that actually matters.

Run notes — 2026-05-12 AM

  • 4 candidates this run, below the 6–9 target. Tuesday morning feed thin in the CHRO / direct-buyer slot — consistent with the recent pattern flagged in 2026-05-11 PM. Quality pick is Deb D. carrying the run on her own, with three lower-engagement fresh-window candidates rounding out the set.
  • Strongest pick: Deb D. (The TCM Group). 39 reactions, 3d old. Reflection from a People Leader magazine interview reframing L&D away from one-off tick-box training toward embedding development into everyday leadership behaviour. Exact Real Talk thesis from a UK people-consultancy figure. Comment section worth a Track-2 pass on the next run — Danny Wareham (UK L&D voice) in the reactions list signals the kind of practitioner audience showing up.
  • Audience B candidate: Marco B. CAMS Financial Crime Specialist running the CDD-as-checkbox vs CDD-as-risk-assurance frame. 3 reactions, 47m old. Below the typical engagement floor but practitioner-language and the explicit compliance-theatre vs risk-assurance distinction make it an unusually early-window pick. The post is also reposted under his FinCrime Agent brand page.
  • Audience C extras: Alex Austin and Asha Berzon. Both fresh, both below the 25-reaction floor, both running framings that map directly onto Real Talk's pitch (NFM behaviourally-ready-not-policy-ready, and MHAW manager training gap). Including them as early-reply moves rather than high-engagement stage plays.
  • CHRO bucket empty this run. Path 5 active-buyer searches dry again (any-recommendations manager training surfaced only the Grace Kelly post already logged yesterday plus off-target Pakistani/Indian job-seeker content; looking-for platform e-learning surfaced Indonesian hiring posts; looking-for leadership programme/training surfaced an INSEAD healthcare leadership programme retrospective and a reporting webinar). Audience A Path 1 / Path 2 keyword searches (workplace investigation lessons, employee relations / ER casework HR, first-time manager training, learning transfer, knowing-doing gap, tick-box training, Worker Protection HR Director) all returned off-ICP, recruitment-focused, or duplicate content from previous runs.
  • Duplicates skipped: Yolanda Pemberton (now posting daily on the NWAF Worker Protection Act theme, fifth post in the 7-day window — remains on the daily-cadence publisher flag, re-pick available from next week). Annie Esler / Optimise Performance (already in last week's data-ids).
  • Near-misses flagged: Tracey West (Purple Hat Coaching, Thrive&Grow Index post, 2nd-degree, exec coach, 0–1 reactions); Daniel Hirschfield (Taylor Wessing, breaking news on the Financial Services Bill / King's Speech preview, 3m old, 0 reactions — the FSMA reform is worth tracking for follow-on Audience C content over the next 48h once law-firm posts react); Alan Ward (Stephenson Harwood, NFM regime piece, 6m old, 0–1 reactions); Kishore Kumar R. (Capital Markets & Regulatory Change Advisor, supervisory-risk paper on Basel/OSFI/ECB appetite, 13m old, 1 reaction — rich content but very banking-CRO/board niche, worth following his account); Sue Conboy (Transformational Coach, MHAW internal-action piece, 0–1 reactions); Wee Siang LIM (typhoon-storytelling, 27 reactions but off-ICP); Qasim Ahmed (AudioFlow AI — competitor in the AI-roleplay-for-workplace-conversations category; not a lead, worth logging the launch in the competitive-watch).
  • Comment-mining opportunity for next run: The TCM Group Deb D. post comment section — Danny Wareham reaction in particular suggests this is a hub L&D thread.
  • King's Speech / FS Bill watch (Wednesday 13 May 2026): per Daniel Hirschfield's pre-announcement post, the Bill is expected to cover removal of the certification regime from SMCR with the FCA/PRA developing a more flexible replacement, plus FOS reform. This is the kind of policy moment that drives a wave of law-firm and consultancy thought leadership over 24–72h — tomorrow's run should specifically scan for first-reaction posts from Lewis Silkin, Pinsent Masons, Dentons UK, Hogan Lovells, Norton Rose Fulbright, Bates Wells and the SMCR-focused boutiques (Auxillias already posted today, worth tracking for follow-ups).
  • Default link format: people-search URLs as per skill instruction.

2026-05-11 (Monday PM) — small but high-quality afternoon run: a clean Path 5 active-buyer (HR Director CEME at Kingspan publicly shopping for change management training), a UK FS practitioner pulling NFM-relevant FCA whistleblowing data, and a 32-reaction Audience C stage from Hacking HR founder Enrique Rubio on AI readiness with the capabilities-versus-skills line that lands exactly where Real Talk does. Path 5 has finally surfaced material after three dry runs.

HR Director CEME at Kingspan Insulated Panels · Audience A (Path 5 active-buyer — publicly asking for change management training recommendations across CEME)

Role
HR Director Continental Europe & Middle East (CEME) at Kingspan Insulated Panels · FTSE 250 building materials manufacturer with 18,000+ employees and a clear post-Grenfell governance overhaul mandate · direct Audience A sign-off persona at the HR Director sign-off level rather than the champion layer beneath
Post topic
Short public-shopping post: “Hello, I’m looking for good company delivering change management training in english. Risk Assessment Manager & Trade Union Representative at Barclays · Audience B (UK FS practitioner pulling FCA Q1 2026 whistleblowing data tied to the September 2026 NFM rules)

Risk Assessment Manager at Barclays plus Trade Union Representative · UK FS practitioner inside one of the largest SMCR-regulated banks · sits at the intersection of conduct risk and worker representation — an unusual but rich angle on NFM enforcement

Pulls the latest FCA quarterly whistleblowing data (Q1 2026) and surfaces the top allegations to make the case that the September 2026 changes treating workplace culture as a conduct risk are clearly evidence-led: #2 Leadership & Senior Managers (Behaviour, Conduct & Integrity, 173 reports); #4 Individual(s): Conduct, Honesty, Integrity & Reputation (98); #5 Firm(s) Values & Integrity (54); #7 Non-Financial Misconduct (39). Implicit thesis: the regulator has the data, the rules are responding to it, and firms still treating this as a training problem are reading it wrong.
