3. Enrique Rubio

Founder, Hacking HR · Top 100 Global HR Influencer · Audience C (high-reach HR community founder on AI readiness, capability vs skill, and training rosters that fill while output stays flat)
Influencer TODO
Role
Founder of Hacking HR (one of the largest global HR practitioner communities) · Top 100 Global HR Influencer · HRE 2024 Top 100 HR Tech Influencer · 2nd-degree connection · Audience C stage with a comment section heavy in HR and people leaders
Post topic
Launch post for the SAIL (Strategic AI Leadership in Business) guide. Walks through seven components of the workplace ecosystem, four layers of readiness (technical / human / cultural / strategic), and the AI literacy vs fluency distinction. The line that lands hardest: “Capabilities versus skills. A skill belongs to a person. A capability belongs to an organisation. Confusing them is why training rosters fill while output stays flat.” Closes with the three layers of AI use (efficiency / augmentation / transformation) and the point that most firms operate at efficiency and call it transformation. Exact Real Talk thesis from a high-reach voice on AI in HR.
Engagement
32 reactions · 4 comments · 2 reposts · ~1d old, still climbing · clears the Audience C 25-reaction floor · comment section is HR practitioners debating readiness frameworks — worth a Track-2 pass next run
Post age
~1d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Brilliant teardown. The capabilities versus skills line is gold, training rosters fill while output stays flat is exactly where most AI readiness budgets quietly die. What are you seeing actually move teams from efficiency into augmentation?
Connection request
Hi Enrique, really enjoyed your SAIL guide post on AI readiness. Would love to swap notes on what’s worked when the gap is capability rather than skill, especially for HR teams trying to get past efficiency-stage AI.

Run notes — 2026-05-11 PM

  • 3 candidates this run, below the 6–9 target. Monday afternoon feed stayed thin — same dynamic the AM run flagged. Quality over quantity called for; the three picks each carry the run on their own merits rather than padding with weak Audience B/C content.
  • Strongest pick — and the headline of the run: Grace Kelly. HR Director CEME at Kingspan Insulated Panels (FTSE 250, 18,000+ employees) publicly asking for change management training recommendations. Path 5 active-buyer at the HR Director sign-off level, comment section already filling with vendor recommendations — getting in early with a reframe (managers actually holding the change conversation under pushback) is the play. This is the first clean Path 5 hit after the AM run flagged three consecutive dry runs — the “any recommendations manager training” search did the work.
  • Second-strongest: Chris Rowse — Risk Assessment Manager + Trade Union Representative at Barclays, pulling FCA Q1 2026 whistleblowing data with the top categories that directly map to the September 2026 NFM rules. 3 reactions and 1h old, but the data pull and the cross-perspective (conduct risk function + TU rep inside Barclays) make this an unusually rich early-window candidate. The 173 leadership-behaviour reports number is one Toby could carry into multiple Audience B conversations from here.
  • Audience C: Enrique Rubio — Hacking HR founder and Top 100 Global HR Influencer launched his SAIL (Strategic AI Leadership) guide with the exact Real Talk frame: capabilities versus skills, training rosters fill while output stays flat, efficiency vs augmentation vs transformation. 32 reactions / 4 comments / 2 reposts, comment section is HR practitioners — worth a Track-2 pass on the next run to surface direct Audience A leads.
  • Path 5 hit-rate — finally not dry. The “any recommendations manager training” query returned Grace Kelly cleanly; the rest of the Path 5 cluster (looking for e-learning platform, AI roleplay recommendation, competitor-mention) returned animation portfolios, EU NGO content, and Indian/Pakistani openings. Worth keeping the “any recommendations + manager / change / leadership training” phrasing on the daily rotation — it’s the highest-yield Path 5 query we’ve seen this month.
  • Audience B Track 1 thin again. Three lines of defence threw off Qatar GRC consultant adverts and German AML event recaps. EHRC / preventive duty returned HSE software and recruitment posts. Compliance theatre returned an Andrew Walker (Protocol Collective) vendor risk register post and a Cape Town data classification piece — both off-ICP. Practitioner-language searches are increasingly the only route to clean Audience B leads.
  • Duplicates skipped: Yolanda Pemberton has now posted four times in eight days on the Worker Protection Act / sexual harassment theme. Today’s Substack publication (7h, 1 reaction, 1 comment) is fresh content but she remains inside the 7-day window from previous picks — flag her as a daily-cadence publisher and consider re-picking next week, the NWAF positioning is consistently exact-fit. Andrew Walker / Protocol Collective vendor risk register post: thematically on-brand for compliance theatre framing but 0 visible reactions, 22m old, fails the Audience C 25-reaction floor.
  • Near-misses flagged: Paul Westlake (Digital Learning Director, “If your compliance or leadership training is a tick-box exercise, it might be time to rethink the approach” — exact-fit frame but 2 reactions and a self-promo close); Robert Glynn (Skillcast vendor, 5-minute training framing, low engagement); Heather Moore (47-reaction NHS workforce piece, off-ICP but worth tracking as a thought leader); Steve Phillip (Mental Health Awareness Week / suicide prevention, 42 reactions but off-ICP).
  • Comment-mining opportunity for next run: Enrique Rubio’s SAIL guide post — HR practitioners in the comments are the highest-yield Audience A reservoir we’ve seen this week. Run a Track-2 pass on the comment section first thing tomorrow.
  • Default link format: people-search URLs as per skill instruction.

Debut of a weekly practitioner-view series on the FCA’s 2026/27 work programme: NFM rules taking effect September 2026 with bullying / harassment / discrimination embedded in Conduct Rules and F&P; FCA’s compulsory AML questionnaire (Nov 2025) and MLRO interviews stress-testing whether AML controls are genuinely fit for purpose; Consumer Duty moving from embedding to evidencing (“show me what’s changed for your customers”); AI governance window closing. Closes with: the real question isn’t whether firms can keep up with the rules, it’s whether the culture underneath the compliance infrastructure is strong enough to hold.

“I Build AI-Enabled Learning Systems That Drive Enterprise Performance” · LxD / Enablement / Transformation · WISF Power 100 Honoree · Audience C (high-engagement LXD vs ID thesis post that frames AI in L&D exactly the way Real Talk does — rich comment section for Track-2 mining)

Post topic
Sharp piece on the difference between an Instructional Designer (using AI to draft scripts, generate quizzes, summarise SME content, speed up course production — AI as production accelerator) and a Learning Experience Designer / Architect (using AI to map performance gaps, align learning to business KPIs, build full learning ecosystems and AI coaches, connect LMS / HRIS / CRM, use data to continuously improve behaviour and adoption — AI as strategic infrastructure). Closes with: “Content is not learning, and learning is not performance. You can prompt your way to a course, but you cannot prompt your way to a high-performing organisation.” Exact Real Talk thesis from a high-reach voice.

4. Buck Rogers

Cybersecurity Advisor & Professor · Operational Resilience, Threat Intelligence & Modern Assurance · Audience C (cyber / op-resilience commentary on TIBER-EU framework drifting into compliance theatre — framing carries directly into the people-risk side of Real Talk’s pitch)
Influencer TODO
Role
Cybersecurity advisor and professor · specialism in operational resilience, threat intelligence and modern assurance · UK financial sector background (CBEST origin work) · Audience C stage with a comment section spanning CISOs, op-resilience leads and risk practitioners
Post topic
Tracks the evolution from CBEST to the ECB’s updated TIBER-EU framework for intelligence-led resilience testing. Argues the important shift was never just “do more red teaming” — it was the move to intelligence-led scenarios, realistic adversary behaviour, testing live operational resilience, assessing people / process / technology together, executive decision-making under pressure, and whether critical services actually survive disruption. Closes with: the challenge now is ensuring these exercises remain meaningful and do not slowly drift into compliance theatre. Real resilience is built through realism, preparation, difficult conversations, trusted relationships, and calm decision-making when things become uncertain.
Engagement
5 reactions · ~45m old, still climbing · below the typical Audience C 25-reaction floor but the framing maps directly onto Real Talk’s wheelhouse (executive decision-making under pressure / difficult conversations) and the post is at the start of its life
Post age
~45m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Sharp read. The drift-into-compliance-theatre bit is the quiet failure mode, the framework lands fine in the room and the executive decision under pressure is the moment you find out if any of it stuck. How are you seeing firms keep the exercises honest?
Connection request
Hi Buck, sharp read on the CBEST to TIBER-EU piece. Would love to swap notes on how firms keep intelligence-led testing from drifting into compliance theatre, the executive-under-pressure moments are where it usually shows.

Group Marketing Director · “Not Leadership” newsletter — Leadership without the corporate BS · Audience C (manager-development thought leader on the three walls every first-time manager hits in their first 90 days — Real Talk-adjacent voice with a growing newsletter audience)

Role
Group Marketing Director and author of the “Not Leadership” newsletter (“real talk, practical tools, better teams”) · UK-flavoured manager-development voice for newly promoted leaders · 2nd-degree connection · Audience C with reach into first-time managers and the L&D / people-leader peers around them
Post topic
The three walls every first-time manager hits in their first 90 days, framed as three honest gut-check questions: (1) are you still doing 40% of your old job at evenings and weekends; (2) do you end most days wondering what you actually got done; (3) have you stopped saying “I don’t know yet” because you think it makes you look weak. Newsletter piece walks through all three and the five small moves that get you past them.
Engagement
0 reactions visible · ~45m old, still climbing · below the typical Audience C floor but the Real Talk-adjacent thesis (rehearsal vs reality, the new role not starting until the old one ends) and the newsletter readership make this a useful stage to be early on
Post age
~45m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Really useful framing. The still-doing-40%-of-your-old-job wall is the one most new managers don’t realise they’re hitting, the new role hasn’t started yet because the old one hasn’t ended. What’s worked for you to break it?
Connection request
Hi Laura, really enjoyed your three-walls piece for first-time managers. Would love to swap notes on what you’ve seen actually help new managers stop carrying their old job into the new one in the first 90 days.

The closing thesis — the real question isn’t whether firms can keep up with the rules, it’s whether the culture underneath the compliance infrastructure is strong enough to hold — is exact-fit Audience B territory. Engagement is 1 reaction (1h old) but the practitioner debut format and thesis match make this a high-quality early-window pick.
  • Second-strongest: Gabrielle P. — 392 reactions / 46 comments / 55 reposts on a high-reach LXD-vs-ID-with-AI post that closes with “content is not learning, and learning is not performance.” Direct Real Talk thesis from a senior L&D voice; comment section is dense with Heads of L&D and learning architects — worth Track-2 mining on the next run for direct Audience A leads.
  • Co-build sighting — not a candidate. Mitzi Danielson-Kaslik MICA CG (1st degree) posted a Real Talk-tagged AML scenario piece (1h old, 5 reactions, 2 comments): “A lot of compliance training is a waste of time… I’ve been building this with Toby Sinclair — AI-led roleplay for real-world client scenarios.” Mitzi is an active Real Talk collaborator rather than a prospect — logged here so Toby can react / repost directly outside the tracker if he wants the air cover for the comment section.
  • Path 5 active-buyer searches dry for the third consecutive run. Today’s Path 5 queries (any recommendations training / e-learning / LMS, AI roleplay or conversation simulation, manager training recommendation) returned a Cuebo sales-roleplay vendor roundup, a Golden Rabbit AI memory-architecture paper, Indian L&D vendor pitches and maritime simulation content. No UK practitioner shopping posts. Worth running the competitor-mention cluster (Articulate / Bodyswaps / Synthesia / Vyond alternative OR vs) on the next run to test whether comparison-shoppers surface mid-week.
  • Audience B Track 1 thin. Practitioner-language searches (three lines of defence, compliance theatre, FCA enforcement, Consumer Duty) returned mostly South African GRC templates, a Saudi RMFS certification post and Compliance Consultant’s fair-value-framework promo. The strongest Audience B pickup was Oyin Sotoyinbo (above), found via the NFM search.
  • Duplicates skipped: Yolanda Pemberton (two new Worker Protection Act / sexual harassment posts but still inside the 7-day window), Annie Esler (also still inside the window), Chantal Cross (sexual harassment prevention specialist with a low-engagement Ofsted presentation today, not strong enough to override the freshness rule). Worth flagging Yolanda for re-pick on next mid-week run if she continues to publish at this cadence — the NWAF positioning is consistently exact-fit.
  • Near-misses flagged: Shrutika J. (HR Professional with an “L&D programs designed to make HR look busy” post — exact Real Talk thesis but 0 reactions, 14m old, 3rd-degree, India-based); Scott Murray (consultant on public sector management/accountability crisis — sharp framing but 0 reactions); Dean Rigby and Steve Conley (adviser-firm AI-iceberg posts — Consumer Duty-adjacent but more about admin automation than people-risk territory); Paris Koutaras (cyber/IT support manager on security training matching real working conditions — exact-fit framing but cyber not direct ICP).
  • Audience C comment-mining opportunity: Gabrielle P.’s LXD vs ID post (46 comments, dense with L&D practitioners) and Stephenson Harwood’s Association of Foreign Banks NFM workshop recap (5 reactions but comments restricted — mine the AFB page reactions instead for UK FS HR / compliance leads) are both worth Track-2 passes on the next run.
  • Default link format: people-search URLs as per skill instruction. Toby clicks through from search results to the right profile in one step.

2026-05-10 (Sunday Mother’s Day) — quiet day across the feed: 1st-degree Audience A practitioner on training rollout patterns, 1st-degree Audience B audit-committee reporting / compliance theatre, plus Audience C stages on Worker Protection Act third-party harassment, Dentons v SRA AML threshold, and first-time managers without reps

1. Nigel Laing

Learning systems for safety-critical work / Frontline leadership development · Audience A (Path 4 status-quo critique — L&D leader publicly identifying that organisations optimise for deployment speed not learning) · 1st degree to Toby
CHRO TODO
Role
Senior L&D leader specialising in safety-critical work, competency-based apprenticeships, Indigenous workforce partnership and frontline leadership development · currently open to corporate and portfolio-level roles · direct Audience A persona · 1st-degree connection
Post topic
Sharp pattern-recognition piece on what an LMS rollout reveals about how an organisation thinks about learning. Modules assigned across the org before anyone has mapped which roles need which content, completions don’t register so people redo them and use screenshots as verification, site champions designated without being told they’re champions, completion contests launched before bugs resolved. Closes with: “Deploying training and enabling learning aren’t the same thing. Organizations that treat them as the same will repeat this pattern with every new system.” Direct Real Talk thesis from a senior L&D voice.
Engagement
1 reaction · still climbing · below the typical Audience A engagement floor but the 1st-degree relationship and exact-fit thesis make this a high-quality candidate even with low reactions
Post age
~15h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Really useful read. The screenshots-as-verification bit is the moment most rollouts give the game away, the system is doing its job, the org just optimised for the wrong outcome. How are you seeing teams catch that earlier?
Connection request
Hi Nigel, really useful read on the training-rollout-as-mirror piece. Would love to swap notes on what you’ve found actually shifts orgs from optimising for deployment to optimising for whether the right people learned the right thing.

2. James Walker

Global Head of Audit / NED / Board Advisor / Fractional Executive Director (CEO, COO, CCO, CAE) · Audience B (UK audit + compliance leadership voice, FCCA + CMIIA + Chartered MCSI) · 1st degree to Toby · post on what separates useful internal audit reporting from compliance theatre
ICP TODO
Role
Global Head of Audit, Non-Executive Director, Board Advisor and Fractional Executive Director (CEO / COO / CCO / CAE) · positioned for Chief Auditor, NED and Chair roles · UK audit + risk + compliance peer at the most senior end · 1st-degree connection · direct Audience B persona
Post topic
An internal audit report should help an audit committee answer one question: are we operating within the risk appetite we set, and if not, what are we doing about it? “If the report does not change a decision, it is just documentation.” Walks through EY’s 2025 Audit Committee Guide section 6.6.5 (findings unfiltered by management, clear rating scale, root cause, practical recommendations) and identifies three disciplines that separate useful reporting from compliance theatre. Closes with: “Does the audit committee read this report and do something different? If not, the report was never the problem.”
Engagement
2 reactions · 1 comment · still climbing · below the typical Audience B engagement floor but the 1st-degree relationship and exact-fit topic make this a high-quality candidate even at this engagement level
Post age
~3h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Spot on. The does-this-change-a-decision test is the cleanest filter, the report is doing its job or it’s documentation. How are you seeing audit teams build that habit in?
Connection request
Hi James, really enjoyed the audit-reporting piece. Would love to swap notes on the does-this-change-a-decision test, especially in lean or co-sourced functions where the temptation to over-engineer for credibility is highest.

3. Paul M.

Chartered FCIPD HR Consultant & HR Director · Employment Law / Employee Relations / Change Management / HR Strategy / International HR · Audience C (UK HR consultant pitching annual anti-harassment review off the back of the third-party harassment / 25% uplift changes)
Influencer TODO
Role
Chartered FCIPD HR Consultant and HR Director · UK SME / scale-up HR advisory practice · Audience C voice with a practitioner client base of HR Directors and people leads in client-facing businesses
Post topic
Worker Protection Act 2024 set the original positive duty; the Employment Rights Act 2025 raises the bar further. Two changes most employers haven’t internalised: (1) employer liability for third-party harassment is reinstated — client / contractor / member of the public, you can be liable even though you don’t employ the harasser; (2) tribunals can uplift compensation by 25% if all reasonable preventative steps weren’t taken. Pitches an annual anti-harassment review (risk assessment, policy & ops review, training) for any business with client-facing or public-facing staff.
Engagement
5 reactions · 2 comments · clears the bar for an Audience C consultant stage · comment section is HR practitioners and SME leaders
Post age
~3d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Really useful breakdown. The 25% uplift bit is going to bite hardest where the annual review exists on paper but managers haven’t actually rehearsed the third-party intervention under real pressure. What’s worked for you on that?
Connection request
Hi Paul, really useful breakdown on the Employment Rights Act and the third-party harassment uplift. Would love to swap notes on how you’re getting client-facing managers to actually practise the intervention before the rules get tested in real time.

4. Bryan Johnson

Founder, Search The Law Group · Building searchthe.law (free AI-powered UK legal research, 129K+ Court Judgments Indexed) · Audience C (UK legal-tech / regulatory commentary stage on the new Dentons v SRA AML seriousness threshold — useful framing for Audience B comment-mining and law-firm Toby pitches)
Influencer TODO
Role
Founder of Search The Law Group, building searchthe.law — a free AI-powered UK legal research platform indexing 129K+ Court Judgments · UK regulatory / professional standards commentary · reach into COLPs, MLROs and law-firm risk leads
Post topic
Court of Appeal ruling Dentons v SRA [2026] EWCA Civ 508 establishes that breaching the Money Laundering Regulations does not automatically cross the line into disciplinary territory — there is now a binding seriousness threshold (paragraph 120: would competent and reputable solicitors consider the conduct serious enough to be categorised as misconduct?). Rejects SRA’s “grammatical interpretation” of Principle 7 as strict liability. Case goes back to SDT on the AML charge. Notes the SRA’s February 2026 sanctions guidance is raising the bar for what “competent and reputable” looks like.
Engagement
1 reaction · 1h old · still climbing · specialist legal-tech audience so engagement floor is lower than mainstream practitioner content; topic is exact fit for Audience B comment-mining
Post age
~1h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Sharp read. The shift from grammatical-Principle-7 to a competent-and-reputable seriousness test puts a lot more weight on whether the firm’s people would actually have spotted and escalated the bad facts. How are you seeing COLPs prep for that?
Connection request
Hi Bryan, sharp read on the Dentons v SRA piece. Would love to swap notes on what you’re seeing COLPs do to actually evidence the competent-and-reputable bar, the policies are usually fine, the practitioner judgment moments are where it lands or doesn’t.

5. Jeff Pierce

Coach / consultant focused on “fixing weak accountability in new managers” · Audience C (manager-development thought leader on the no-reps-no-roadmap problem with first-time managers)
Influencer TODO
Role
Manager-development coach / consultant whose positioning is explicitly “fixing weak accountability in new managers” · runs newsletter and content on first-time-manager onboarding · reaches HR / L&D and people-manager audiences
Post topic
LaGuardia approach analogy: the hard stuff (delivering difficult feedback, dealing with the teammate who quits before a deadline) never goes away — experience just means you stop fumbling for your phone and become present with the discomfort. The problem is that most first-time managers don’t get those early reps in a structured way with guidance and a roadmap; “they get thrown into the seat and expected to figure out the view on their own.” Damage shows up as poor retention, HR complaints and culture issues; the hidden loss is the missed opportunity of helping a former top performer make a big impact.
Engagement
8 reactions · 5 comments · clears the bar for a manager-development thought-leader stage · comment section is L&D and people-manager peers
Post age
~2d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Really useful read. The thrown-into-the-seat bit is where most of the early damage happens, the first awkward conversation lands without any reps and the new manager either avoids it or fumbles through. How are you seeing the best ones get those reps in?
Connection request
Hi Jeff, really enjoyed the first-time-managers-no-reps piece. Would love to swap notes on what you’ve found actually gives new managers structured reps before they have to handle the first awkward conversation live.

Run notes — 2026-05-10 (Sunday Mother’s Day)

  • 5 candidates this run, below the 6–9 target. Sunday Mother’s Day — the LinkedIn feed across all three audiences was dominated by personal / holiday content and recruitment ads. Quality over quantity called for; the strongest two candidates are 1st-degree connections that don’t need to clear the usual engagement threshold.
  • Strongest pick: Nigel Laing — 1st-degree L&D leader specialising in safety-critical work and frontline leadership development. His “Deploying training and enabling learning aren’t the same thing” thesis is exact-fit Real Talk territory and he’s currently open to corporate / portfolio roles. Engagement is 1 reaction (15h old) but the 1st-degree relationship plus thesis match make this an unusually high-quality candidate even at low engagement.
  • Second-strongest: James Walker — 1st-degree Global Head of Audit / NED. The “does-this-change-a-decision” test piece on internal audit reporting vs compliance theatre is exact-fit for Audience B and the 1st-degree relationship makes the comment land warmer than a cold influencer post.
  • Path 5 active-buyer searches dry again. Three Path 5 queries today (any recommendations e-learning OR LMS, Synthesia alternative OR vs comparison, AI roleplay OR AI role-play) returned only generic noise — MATLAB hobbyists, US LMS admin job posts, and an Indian crypto debrief. No practitioner shopping posts surfaced. This is the second consecutive run with Path 5 dry; flagging for re-prioritisation. Worth running competitor-mention searches (Articulate / Bodyswaps / Vyond alternative) on the next mid-week run when LinkedIn engagement returns to baseline.
  • Audience B Track 1 thin. Practitioner-language searches (three lines of defence, FCA enforcement / fine, section 166, compliance theatre / fatigue, NFM / COCON) all returned cybersecurity / AI-governance content rather than UK FS practitioner posts. The strongest pickup was James Walker (above) — routed to ICP/Audience B based on his Chartered MCSI / FCCA / CMIIA credentials and the audit-committee framing.
  • Duplicates skipped: Yolanda Pemberton (still on May 5), Annie Esler (May 9 AM), Chris Nagel (May 9 PM), Pam Loch (May 9 PM), Nic Elliott (April 29). Nic Elliott has a strong new post (HR teams dealing with AI grievances, 204 reactions, 21 comments, 1st degree) but is inside the 7-day duplicate window — flag for next week if he stays in feed.

Worth running again next session as Toby noted these are highest-conversion when they hit.

Estimates ERA will cost UK business £1bn annually.

Argues the recurring problem in engagement surveys often traces back to a first-time manager asking for feedback and then defending themselves not aggressively, not obviously, but enough that the employee silently decides never again to say anything that creates that experience. Decision doesn’t show up in the engagement data for months. Frames it as a learnable skill, not a character flaw. Sharp diagnosis of the moment trust quietly leaks under managerial inexperience.

Contrasts vague (“you’ve seemed a bit off lately”) against specific (“twice this week the update landed after the agreed time, and yesterday the client had to chase for an answer”). Lands on: most managers don’t need to sound tougher, they need to sound clearer. Clean frame for the same wobble Real Talk Studio is built around.

Run notes — 2026-05-07

  • ~14 keyword searches across the three audiences. Distribution: ~8 Audience A (knowing-doing gap; difficult conversations manager; Worker Protection Act all reasonable steps; tick box compliance; manager capability development; Employment Rights Act HR; first-time manager training; performance conversation manager; mandatory training doesn’t work; line manager capability gap; accidental manager); ~3 Audience B (non-financial misconduct; three lines of defence compliance; SMCR conduct rules; Consumer Duty year 2 board; Head of Compliance FCA; COCON bullying); ~3 Audience C (Lewis Silkin OR Browne Jacobson harassment; CIPD research; AI roleplay; FCA Dear CEO).
  • Audience A (CHRO/HR practitioner) and Audience B (Compliance ICP) both came back THIN today — the strongest UK practitioner voices in our last-7-days data have already been featured in the past week (Julie Darmudas re-surfaced with the same NFM PS26/6 post she ran yesterday; Kim Smart re-surfaced on Consumer Duty Year 2 board; Lindsey Alt re-surfaced on Virta connective tissue; Yolanda Pemberton re-surfaced on Worker Protection Act). All four were skipped to avoid duplicate engagement in the same week. As a result, today’s candidates are 7/7 Audience C. Note for the next run: rotate to Path-1 evidence and Path-2 transfer-of-learning sub-clusters to surface a fresh practitioner pool.
  • Strongest pick: Lee Chambers at 17 reactions / 9 comments / 2 reposts on the National Allyship Summit launch teaser — FTSE Women Leaders Review and KPMG-supported, with sponsor list (Scottish Widows, IKEA, Lewis Silkin, NatWest, ScottishPower, Browne Jacobson) that mirrors our Tier 1 industries. High-quality CHRO/people-leader audience in the comment thread; ideal stage for Toby’s practising-it-in-the-moment frame.
  • Second pick: Thomas Contin (8 reactions / 7 comments) on MiCA Articles 68–72 governance — the “three lines on paper but lacking genuine independence” line is precisely Real Talk Studio’s evidence-trail territory, and the comment section is a near-certain Track-2 source for in-house compliance/risk practitioners. Worth re-scanning the comments in 24–48h.
  • Path-2 (manager development) candidates clustered today around the accidental-manager / first-time-manager scenario — Andy Goram (podcast with Dawn Stallwood), Sarika Goel (feedback shutdown), Joshua Felton (performance-conversation specificity), and Mike Hood (ERA 2025 line manager prep as the third priority). All Audience C, all UK. Engagement is below the 25-reaction threshold for most, but content quality is high and the topic spread shows strong category gravity for Real Talk Studio in the next 1–2 weeks.
  • Repeat-author skips this run: Julie Darmudas (PS26/6 SUP15 vs REP008 NFM — same post she ran 2026-05-06), Kim Smart (Consumer Duty Year 2 board challenge — already 2026-05-06), Lindsey Alt (Virta connective tissue — already 2026-05-06-pm), Yolanda Pemberton (NWAF Worker Protection Act — already 2026-05-05).

AI roleplay tools post (sales-focused vendor pitch, low signal value despite Path-3 keyword match).
  • Watchlist scan: Lewis Silkin surfaced today only as a Workshop Sponsor reference (Building Respect / Construction conference) and as a Lee Chambers Summit sponsor — no original Lewis Silkin partner post in the past 24h. Browne Jacobson similarly only surfaced as a Silver Sponsor of the Allyship Summit. CIPD research search returned mostly non-CIPD content. No Big-4 (Deloitte / PwC / KPMG / EY) practitioner-named posts surfaced this run.

2026-05-06 (evening scan) — compliance-training-by-button-mash design failure, compliance fatigue when everything is critical, CHRO hiring brief and the psychological-courage gap

1. Gemma de Laat

Training, Documentation & Enablement Leader · Path 4 (status-quo / solution-aware) · substantive in-house L&D / instructional-design take on why mandatory compliance training fails — the “design signalled it’s a hoop” framing exactly matches the Real Talk Studio thesis
CHRO TODO
Role
Training, Documentation & Enablement Leader · Instructional Design / Knowledge Management / Process Optimisation · Startups & Scale-Ups · Path 2 / Path 4 in-house L&D practitioner who designs the actual modules
Post topic
A friend finished a week’s worth of mandatory training in one morning by button-mashing past videos, skipping to the knowledge checks and eliminating obviously-wrong answers by instinct. Argues this isn’t a learner attitude problem, it’s the design signalling clearly that the training is a hoop to jump through, so the learner treats it like one. The fix isn’t making videos unskippable, it’s designing training that respects the learner enough they’d actually want to complete it. Direct critique of the Layer-1 compliance-training status quo.
Engagement
6 reactions · healthy for a substantive practitioner critique within the past day
Post age
~10h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Spot on. The button-mash bit is the design honestly signalling to her what the org actually values, and she responded rationally to that. How are you measuring whether your redesigned compliance training shifts behaviour, not just completion?
Connection request
Hi Gemma, really useful post on the friend who button-mashed a week of mandatory training in one morning. Would love to swap notes on what’s working when you redesign compliance training so the design itself stops signalling it’s just a hoop.

1. Lindsey Alt

Learning, Leadership Development & OD Leader at Virta Health · Path 2 (L&D / Leadership Development champion) · substantive in-house practitioner take on why leadership development doesn’t stick — the “connective tissue” framing and a shared Leadership Blueprint as the language layer underneath programmes
CHRO TODO
Role

Post topic
“When leadership development doesn’t stick, the content usually isn’t the problem — the problem is connective tissue.” Frames the failure mode as people leaving a learning experience and walking back into an environment that reinforces old patterns. The fix she describes is a system that connects development across levels — aspiring leaders, front-line managers, senior leaders — with a shared Leadership Blueprint underneath. Cites a 25% increase in manager confidence across core leadership behaviours one year in. Knowing-doing-gap territory in plain language.

2. Martin Jones

Exec Director, Gee 7 Wealth Management · employee-owned wealth firm · Consumer Duty as a governance test, not a compliance test · MI without interpretation is weak · squarely Audience B
ICP TODO
Role
Exec Director at Gee 7 Wealth Management Limited · UK wealth manager · senior accountable executive at a regulated firm with Consumer Duty exposure — cleanly inside the Audience B (Compliance ICP / sign-off) frame
Post topic
Frames Consumer Duty as a governance test rather than a compliance test now that open products have been live since July 2023 and closed books since July 2024. Argues the real test is whether boards can convincingly explain why their data supports the conclusions they’re drawing. Three board observations: MI without interpretation is weak; responsibility for outcomes can’t be pushed down the chain; evidence of challenge and follow-through matters as much as the data itself. Closes with an open question on moving Consumer Duty from reporting into genuine board-level decision-making — an explicit invitation to a substantive comment.
Engagement
3 reactions · below stage threshold but a substantive senior-practitioner voice with an open question and a precise frame for our pitch
Post age
~3d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Yeah, this is good. The MI rarely fails, the board conversation around it does. What have you found actually shifts that from review into proper challenge?

Frames compliance theatre as the function’s self-inflicted credibility problem.

Infosec & AI Governance Lead at Tetrate · senior practitioner publishing under his own name on UK FS AI governance · speaks the Consumer Duty / supervisory-tool vocabulary natively — cleanly Audience B
Post topic
Argues the FCA AI Live Testing Cohort 2 (Barclays, Experian, Lloyds/Scottish Widows, UBS, GoCardless, Aereve, Coadjute, Palindrome, with Advai as technical partner) is where the de facto UK rulebook for Consumer Duty × AI is actually being written. Notes the deliverable isn’t a rule, a Dear CEO letter, or a thematic review — it’s a non-binding “good and poor practice” report that supervisors will reach for. Smart structural read: the smaller fintechs in the cohort, free of thirty years of model-risk inertia, will set the realistic floor that generalises.
Engagement
1 reaction · below stage threshold but the read is sharp and the audience is exactly our Audience B
Post age
~1d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Sharp read. The good and poor practice format does the supervisory work without firms ever getting to litigate it. How are you seeing firms actually prep for that floor?
Connection request
Hi Paul, sharp read on the FCA AI Live Testing Cohort piece. Would love to hear how you’re seeing firms actually prep for that supervisory floor.

5. Nicola Richardson

Management Mentor · The Manager’s Academy · care-sector-leadership audience · post about a Registered Manager whose “difficult conversations got circled for weeks before she would have them” reframed through neurodiversity · high-engagement stage
Influencer TODO
Role
Founder, The Manager’s Academy · mentor to Registered Managers and senior care leaders · UK care/social-care audience · consistent ICP-relevant publishing on management-under-pressure
Post topic
Tells the story of a CQC-Outstanding Registered Manager three weeks from a performance plan: late supervision notes, unanswered emails, panic at rota changes, “difficult conversations got circled for weeks before she would have them.” Reframes it through possible neurodivergence — one in five people, including managers — and argues for asking why this is hard before reaching for the performance framework. Lands on a peer-leadership pattern (the manager who is brilliant in a crisis but goes quiet when the difficult conversation is needed) that travels well beyond care.
Engagement
21 reactions · 36 comments · clearly above the 25-reaction stage floor in comment volume — an active thread where a peer-leader perspective will be visible
Post age
~5d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Really useful post. The pattern travels well beyond care, most managers haven’t actually rehearsed the conversation before it lands live. What’s worked for you in helping them practise it safely?
Connection request
Hi Nicola, really useful post on the brilliant-in-a-crisis pattern. Would love to compare notes on what’s working when you help managers practise the harder conversations safely.

6. Antonino Meduri

Principal, AM Law & Partners (AU) · Queensland Industrial Relations Commission case · tick-box harassment training fails the “all reasonable steps” defence · modules “could be skipped or gamed” · the precise UK Worker Protection Act dynamic, told from a jurisdiction one cycle ahead
Influencer TODO
Role
Principal at AM Law & Partners · commercial litigation, employment and WHS lawyer · Australia-based but the audience reads UK practitioners watching how reasonable-steps defences fail in adjacent jurisdictions
Post topic
Breaks down a Queensland Industrial Relations Commission case in which the employer’s “all reasonable steps” defence failed despite generic policies, contracts and training modules. The forensic detail is the gold: training could be skipped or gamed (slides bypassed, quizzes retried until correct); modules were completed only every two years and alongside other duties; a 2016 final warning against the same manager did not trigger targeted controls. Lands on three rules the UK is about to live with: reasonable steps requires effective tailored controls, known risks demand targeted intervention, and training must be engaged with, retained and enforced.
Engagement
6 reactions · below 25-reaction stage floor but the topic, the named-judgment specificity and the legal-practitioner audience make this an unusually high-quality stage for a UK-side comment
Post age
~6d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Spot on. The slides bypassed, quizzes retried bit is going to come up loads in tribunals once Worker Protection lands. What are you seeing actually holding up as evidence right now?
Connection request
Hi Antonino, really useful breakdown of the Queensland reasonable steps case. Would love to swap notes on what evidence is actually holding up for employers right now.

7. Megan Duff

Leadership coach for middle managers · LinkedIn Live event “Mastering Hard Conversations: What great managers do differently” (Fri 15 May, 11am) · performance / progression / interpersonal frame · useful stage with an event registration audience
Influencer TODO
Role
Leadership coach · positioning around middle managers · running a free LinkedIn Live on hard conversations — the audience is in-house people-managers and L&D leads, so a comment doubles as visibility into the registration list
Post topic
Promo for a 1-hour LinkedIn Live on 15 May. Frames the avoidance pattern: the performance issue that keeps getting deferred, the colleague dynamic slowly getting worse, the pushback you don’t know how to deliver without damaging your reputation. Names three types of difficult workplace conversation — performance, progression, interpersonal — and promises practical tools and frameworks. The exact “managers know the ones they’ve been avoiding” framing we live next to.
Engagement
14 reactions · 34 attendees registered · below 25-reaction stage floor on reactions but the registered-attendees pool is the real audience — a comment is visible to a self-selected pool of managers who are already looking for help here
Post age
~23h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Really useful framing. The framework lands fine in the room, what doesn’t land is the first attempt under real pressure. How are you weighing rehearsal against frameworks for managers stepping in for the first time?
Connection request
Hi Megan, really useful framing on the three types of hard conversations. Would love to swap notes on rehearsal vs frameworks for managers stepping in for the first time.

2026-05-06 — mixed run: NFM Week-17 policy Monday-morning test, three-lines-of-defence in practice, Consumer Duty Year 3 board challenge, knowing-doing gap stage, compliance-theatre confession

1. Julie Darmudas

Chief Regulatory Officer & MLRO · SMF3 / SMF16 / SMF17 · Chartered HR Practitioner · 30+ Years in Consumer Credit · Path 1 (HR Policy & Compliance bridge) · Week 17 of NFM Countdown post explicitly built around “can a line manager apply this on a Monday morning without calling Legal first?”
CHRO TODO
Role
Chief Regulatory Officer & MLRO at a UK consumer credit firm · SMF3 / SMF16 / SMF17 holder · Chartered HR Practitioner · Board-Level Regulatory Strategy · rare cross-functional Compliance + HR Policy voice (1st-degree connection — warm relationship)
Post topic
Week 17 of NFM Countdown. Frames most NFM policies as “written for a regulator’s eye, not a manager’s desk.” Argues the Monday-morning test is whether a line manager can apply the policy without calling Legal first — if not, “the policy isn’t finished — it’s just documented.” Goes deep on RACI and case-lifecycle ownership: intake → triage → investigation → decision → outcome → MI. Calls out joint accountability as the most common structural failure she sees. This is exactly the Path 1 / HR Policy & Compliance pain point.
Engagement
1 reaction visible · below stage threshold but a substantive, deeply credible practitioner voice and a 1st-degree connection — the comment is the priority, not the audience
Post age
~2d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The Monday-morning test is the right one. What we’re seeing with clients is the policy writes well, the manager guidance writes well, and the line still freezes the first time the conversation actually arrives — tone, escalation timing, what to log. The RACI fixes accountability; the gap underneath is whether anyone has rehearsed the case once before it lands.

2. Tejinder Johal

International Head Legal Entity Compliance Programme · substantive practitioner take on the three lines of defence shifting in practice · activity vs outcomes language · high-quality Audience B voice
ICP TODO
Role
International Head Legal Entity Compliance Programme · senior compliance practitioner at a global / regulated firm · cleanly inside Audience B
Post topic
Argues the three lines of defence model hasn’t changed but how it’s applied has — testing, monitoring and elements of control execution are moving into the first line. Calls out the risk that “the model can start to look right on paper, but behave differently in practice.” The success conditions he names are exactly the territory we work in: first line has the capability not just the accountability, second line still close enough to provide meaningful challenge, shared understanding of where oversight ends and execution begins. This is the design-versus-practice gap framed by a practitioner.
Engagement
6 reactions · below the stage threshold but a substantive practitioner voice and a precise frame for our pitch
Post age
~1d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
“Right on paper, behaves differently in practice” is the line. Most firms I work with are finding that as control execution moves into the first line, the bottleneck is whether front-line managers can actually run the conversation a control depends on — the challenge, the escalation, the documented rationale — under real pressure. The design holds; the capability is what gets tested.

3. Kim Smart MSc

SMF Holder & Data Protection Officer · Advisor on SMF Accountability, Conduct Risk, Financial Crime & Data Governance · Board-Level Regulatory Strategy · sharp post on FCA’s Year 2 Consumer Duty board reports review — activity-vs-outcomes language
ICP TODO
Role
SMF Holder & Data Protection Officer · advises Boards on SMF accountability, conduct risk, financial crime and data governance · UK FS · ideal Audience B persona
Post topic
Reads across the FCA’s Year 2 Consumer Duty Board reports review and frames the shift sharply: “If your Consumer Duty report just gets approved, you’re already behind.” The Year 3 expectation is that Boards challenge the data, form judgements about actual customer outcomes, scrutinise outcomes delivered by intermediaries and third parties, and ask whether customers genuinely understand what’s being sold. “Not: have we signed it off? But: what changed because we didn’t like what we saw?” That’s our pitch in regulator’s language. She’s also visible across the SMCR conversation thread (separate post on PS26/6 Phase 1).
Engagement
8 reactions · below the stage threshold but a high-credibility NED-adjacent voice with a clear point of view
Post age
~22h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
“What changed because we didn’t like what we saw?” is the right test. Most firms I work with can produce the MI; the harder evidence is whether front-line teams actually had a different conversation with a vulnerable customer because of it. The Year 3 board pack that lands well will show outcomes data alongside the actual judgement calls people made under pressure.

4. Catherine McDonald

Organisational Behaviour, Leadership & Lean Coach · LinkedIn Top Voice ‘24, ‘25 & ‘26 · Founder, MCD Consulting · high-engagement post directly on the knowing-doing gap — ideal Audience C stage
Influencer TODO
Role
Founder, MCD Consulting · Organisational Behaviour, Leadership & Lean Coach · LinkedIn Top Voice three years running · Co-Host of Lean Solutions Podcast · large practitioner audience
Post topic
“Is your training investment leaking?” Frames the knowing-doing gap as the reason training spend doesn’t move capability: not embedded in daily work, not supported by systems or habits, not reinforced by leaders consistently. Walks through a 7-step playbook centred on behaviour-based training, short focused sessions, manager involvement and weekly check-ins. The core argument lines up exactly with our pitch: training transfer fails without rehearsal in the actual job. Strong Audience C stage with rich practitioner discussion in the comments worth a Track-2 sweep on a separate pass.
Engagement
117+ reactions · 48 comments · 13 reposts · clearly above the 25+ stage threshold
Post age
~3d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The point about “the organisation isn’t set up to use it” is the one most leadership programmes never recover from. What we’re seeing with clients is that the missing step between training and embedded behaviour is rehearsal — managers practising the actual conversation under pressure before they have to run it for real. Without that, even the tightest 6-session design leaks within a week.

5. Tracy Hall

International Keynote Speaker · Top 20 Fraud Experts on LinkedIn 2025 · Scams & Intimate Fraud · Board Director · raw “compliance training confession” post — gamed it for 25 years and so did her team · high-credibility Audience C stage on Real Talk’s exact territory
Influencer TODO
Role
International Keynote Speaker · Author · LinkedIn Top 20 Fraud Experts 2025 · Scams & Intimate Fraud · Board Director · Mentor · speaks publicly about being targeted by a high-profile con man and turned the experience into educational practice
Post topic
“25 years of annual compliance training was wasted on me. I gamed the system. So did my team. We hated it.” Names the specific failure modes — bad stock imagery, over-engineered scenarios, no behaviour change. Rejects “do more compliance training” as the answer; argues for show-not-tell, embedded learning, fun, tools, progress, encouragement, reward. Reposted by Stacey Edmonds (Lively CEO) with the line “Just say no to compliance theatre!” This is the cleanest practitioner-speaker articulation of our pitch this week.
Engagement
25+ reactions on Tracy’s post · reposted by Stacey Edmonds (Lively, separate stage) · meets the 25+ stage threshold and clearly resonating with the right audience
Post age
~5d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Hard-won is right. The bit most compliance functions still won’t admit is that the people building the training know it’s being gamed too — the completion data just makes it convenient to look the other way. Show-don’t-tell only sticks when people actually rehearse the moment under pressure; until they do, behaviour change is a hope, not a plan.

6. Stacey Edmonds MAICD

Co-Founder & CEO, Lively · cyber-safety / human-risk vendor · reposting Tracy Hall’s compliance-training confession with “Just say no to compliance theatre!” · Audience C peer voice in the same category as Real Talk Studio
Influencer SKIP
Role
Co-Founder & CEO, Lively · building cyber-safety / human-risk training that uses scenario-based practice (Phishy or Not? for businesses, Dodgy or Not? for schools) · Trustworthy AI & social impact · vendor in an adjacent category to ours, posting in the same vocabulary
Post topic
Reposts Tracy Hall’s “25 years of compliance training was wasted on me” confession with the line: “The threat is now painfully real. The solutions need to be too. Just say no to compliance theatre!” Lively’s product narrative is built around exactly the same shift Real Talk argues for: move from passive content to embedded, scenario-based practice that builds protective instinct. A peer / category-adjacent stage rather than a buyer.
Engagement
Audience C: cleanly meets stage criteria as a vendor / category voice with consistent ICP-relevant content; engagement on the repost is modest but the ride-along onto Tracy Hall’s post extends the reach
Post age
~5d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
“Just say no to compliance theatre” lands. The pattern is identical across human-risk categories — cyber, harassment, customer outcomes — the org buys the module, the people game it, and the regulator (or the attacker) eventually finds the seam. The shift that actually moves the dial is rehearsal under realistic pressure, scored on what people actually do, not what they clicked.

2026-05-05 — mixed run: HR practitioner perspective post-EMEA research, NFM/COCON Sept 2026 deep dive, Worker Protection Act vendor stages, knowing-doing gap consultant

1. Flor Farsadi

Global HR Leader · ex-Unilever, Danone, Mitsubishi · conducted 42 interviews with HR Directors across EMEA · conclusion that “organisations are not struggling with strategy — they are struggling with the human side of change” · high-credibility CHRO-adjacent voice
CHRO TODO
Role
Global HR Leader · People & Culture · Organizational Transformation · ex-Unilever, Danone, Mitsubishi · currently between roles, Barcelona-based, EMEA mobility
Post topic
After leaving her last corporate role, completed a Master in Project Management with thesis on AI in Talent Acquisition, ran 42 in-depth interviews with HR Directors across EMEA, taught Change Management and Organisational Transformation at three schools. Bottom-line insight: “organisations are not struggling with strategy. They are struggling with the human side of change. And most organisations are not ready for that conversation.” Now signalling she’s ready to come back inside an organisation. Strong CHRO-adjacent voice with original research evidence, not a vendor pitch.
Engagement
1 reaction · below the stage threshold but a substantive practitioner voice with EMEA HR Director research backing
Post age
~2m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The line that lands is “most organisations are not ready for that conversation.” What we’re seeing with clients is the strategy deck is rarely the bottleneck — it’s whether managers can actually hold the human conversations the strategy depends on. 42 HR Director interviews is a serious evidence base; would be interested in how often that gap surfaced as a capability problem versus a confidence one.

2. Lucia Tudor

Global HR Business Partner · Organizational Design, Workforce Planning & Leadership Advisory across borders · reflective post on the new-manager transition · lists exactly the conversations we target: difficult conversations, real feedback, translating strategy · Path 2 manager-readiness practitioner voice
CHRO TODO
Role
Global HR Business Partner · Organizational Design, Workforce Planning & Leadership Advisory across borders
Post topic
Reflective post on watching strong individual contributors step into people management. Lists what the role actually demands: “translate strategy, have difficult conversations, give real feedback, and learn that your job is no longer to be the smartest in the room. It’s to create the space for others to be.” Frames this as a transition that needs honest self-assessment first, not a default career progression. Genuine HRBP practitioner voice on one of the central pain points the playbook targets.
Engagement
low engagement (early in the post lifecycle) · valuable for the practitioner identity, not the stage
Post age
~12m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The transition framing is exactly right. The bit most organisations skip is rehearsal — the manager nominally understands they need to give real feedback or hold a difficult conversation, but they’ve never done it in a context where the stakes were real and they had time to recover from a misstep. The first time they actually try it tends to be the live one with a struggling team member.

3. Maame Agyarkwa

Senior Compliance Specialist — Global Rates and Retail Structured Products · ex-Global Emerging Markets and Investment Research Coverage · substantive post on the 1 September 2026 COCON expansion citing the FCA’s 2024 wholesale-firm survey · 93 individuals with F&P assessments amended for non-financial misconduct · classic Audience B compliance voice
ICP TODO
Role
Senior Compliance Specialist — Global Rates and Retail Structured Products · ex-Global Emerging Markets and Investment Research Coverage · Building Tech-Enabled, Risk-Intelligent Front Office Solutions
Post topic
From 1 September 2026, bullying a colleague is not just an HR matter — it can be a breach of COCON. Anchors with the FCA’s 2024 wholesale-firm survey: 93 individuals had F&P assessments amended for non-financial misconduct, many of which never reached board level. Three sharp practitioner takeaways: (1) wider scope — client events, conferences, offsites can fall under COCON, (2) a quiet exit no longer a clean slate — serious misconduct must now be disclosed in regulatory references, (3) managers themselves accountable when they’re aware of misconduct and fail to act. Closes: “firms must now treat employees’ behaviour toward each other with the same seriousness as how they behave in markets.”
Engagement
1 reaction · below the stage threshold but high-quality compliance practitioner voice with regulator data backing
Post age
~23h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The third point lands hardest — the manager-aware-and-failed-to-act exposure. What we’re seeing with clients is that most senior populations have never actually rehearsed the moment when they hear about misconduct second-hand and have to decide what to do in the next 48 hours. The policy says escalate; the actual capability is missing. Worth pressure-testing before September.

4. Rachel Yates

Beetlestone Founder & Managing Director · Chartered MCIPD · substantive post tying the HR Magazine article on rising disputes to manager capability gap and Employment Rights Act 2025 · quotes 44% of UK employees experiencing workplace disputes and 500,000+ tribunal backlog · Audience C HR consultant voice with the right framing
Influencer TODO
Role
Founder & MD, Beetlestone HR · Chartered MCIPD · UK SME-focused HR consultancy
Post topic
Reposting an HR Magazine article (“Why fairer workplaces attract more disputes”) with her own framing: 44% of UK employees experiencing workplace disputes, 500,000+ tribunal backlog. Argues the employment landscape has evolved rapidly, employee awareness has increased, but management capability hasn’t kept pace — that gap is where risk sits. Lists the grey areas managers struggle with (robust performance management vs perceived unfairness, direct feedback vs inappropriate behaviour, when a concern becomes a formal grievance) and frames culture, not policy, as the fix. Names the Employment Rights Act 2025 as the trigger leaving leaders second-guessing themselves. Excellent setup for a comment that adds the “rehearsal” angle.
Engagement
0 reactions visible at scan time but very recent post (12m); strong substance and likely to attract HR practitioner comments · Audience C stage candidate
Post age
~12m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Capability is the right frame. The bit I’d push on is that “capability” in most organisations still means a workshop, a framework, and a line in a competency model. What actually closes the gap is rehearsal under pressure — the manager having held the version of the conversation where the employee pushes back, gets emotional, or threatens a grievance, before the real one. That’s what shifts confidence.

5. Yolanda Pemberton

Founder, National Workplace Adjustment Framework (NWAF) · Workplace Accessibility & Governance · two recent posts on Worker Protection Act “all reasonable steps” threshold and a vicarious liability whitepaper · explicit thesis that “traditional HR systems cannot meet this threshold” · Audience C governance/HR consultant stage
Influencer TODO
Role
Founder, National Workplace Adjustment Framework (NWAF)™ · UK governance and accessibility consultant
Post topic
Two-post sequence on Worker Protection Act exposure: (1) the “all reasonable steps” threshold now requires evidence before harm, not after, and traditional HR systems cannot meet it; (2) a 26-page Vicarious Liability & Neuro-conduct Governance whitepaper arguing this is a structural governance issue, not an HR issue. Lists why training becomes “legally stale without cognitive decay monitoring” and how inaccessible grievance routes create structural discrimination. Strong stage for a comment that reframes “all reasonable steps” as evidence-of-capability rather than evidence-of-policy.
Engagement
1 reaction · 1 comment on the whitepaper post · below stage threshold but the framing is on-thesis and the audience (governance leaders, regulators, HR governance teams, legal risk directors) is exactly ours
Post age
1d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
“All reasonable steps” is going to come down to what employers can actually evidence under pressure, not what their policy library contains. What we’re seeing with clients is the gap between “we trained 100% of managers” and “we can show those managers can actually handle the moment” — the second is what a tribunal will care about. The governance-not-HR framing is the right call.

6. Olga Mukhina

Global Leadership Consultant · ex-McKinsey HR · on-thesis post invoking the Knowing–Doing Gap book and arguing leadership programmes still don’t close it · lists exactly the failure modes we cite: no practice, no reflection time, no quality feedback, no accountability · Audience C consultant stage
Influencer TODO
Role
Global Leadership Consultant · High-Performing Teams Coach · Speaker · ex-McKinsey HR professional
Post topic
20+ years on from the Knowing–Doing Gap book, asks why so little has actually changed. Argues knowing has never been easier — doing is still hard. Calls out the typical one-off training pattern: intellectually stimulating sessions, great discussions and energy for two days, nice notebooks full of insights, then back to old habits. Names the missing pieces: practice, reflection time, quality feedback, accountability. Lands on the line: “Leadership doesn’t change through understanding concepts. It changes through practice, dialogue, trying, failing, and adjusting — over time.” Almost a Toby talk-track in someone else’s words — perfect comment surface.
Engagement
1 reaction · below the stage threshold but the framing is so close to ours that the comment can fit naturally without sounding piggy-backy
Post age
~1h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The four missing pieces (practice, reflection, feedback, accountability) is the right diagnosis. The piece I’d add: practice has to be in something close enough to the real moment that the body’s pressure response actually fires, otherwise the “trying, failing, adjusting” loop happens at the wrong level. Most leadership programmes practise the easy version — the live one is where the behaviour actually has to land.

7. Rachael Healey

Partner at Reynolds Porter Chamberlain (RPC) · FCA-regulated firms briefing on the FCA’s AI position · explicitly references Consumer Duty cross-cutting rules, SYSC, SMCR accountability and vulnerable customer guidance as the FCA’s primary AI levers · classic UK law-firm Audience C stage with high ICP-adjacent comment density
Influencer TODO
Role
Partner, Financial Services Regulatory, Reynolds Porter Chamberlain (RPC) · UK law firm watchlist account
Post topic
Weekly “good, bad and ugly for FCA-regulated entities” column. This week: how the FCA is regulating AI without dedicated AI rules — relying instead on Consumer Duty cross-cutting rules, SYSC 4.1.1R, the Senior Managers Regime, vulnerable customer guidance (Principles 8 and 9), and threshold conditions. Notes the FCA explicitly considered a dedicated senior manager for AI and chose not to, relying on the board’s annual Consumer Duty report instead. Concise practitioner-grade FS regulatory briefing.
Engagement
8 reactions · below the 25+ threshold but RPC partner watchlist + likely strong comment density from FS compliance practitioners makes this a Track-2 comment-mining target on its next pass
Post age
~11h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The choice not to create a dedicated AI senior manager and to lean on the existing Consumer Duty board report is telling. What we’re seeing with clients is that the SMF holding the AI risk is increasingly a senior manager who’s never been pressure-tested on AI scenarios — the regulator is going to expect them to evidence active oversight, and most boards aren’t set up to produce that evidence yet.

8. Rupert Hillier

Co-Founder & CEO at Learnlight · on-thesis post on the Peter Principle and the promotion-cycle gap · explicit list of the deeply human capabilities — listening, building trust, coaching under pressure, holding difficult conversations · explicitly calls out generic training as not solving it · Audience C L&D vendor CEO stage
Influencer TODO
Role
Co-Founder & CEO, Learnlight · UK/EU language and skills learning vendor
Post topic
Post on the Peter Principle in real time: the top-performing analyst gets the management role, then is suddenly expected to coach, motivate, develop a team, navigate conflict, hold difficult conversations and keep people engaged through change — and nobody taught them how. Lists the human skills (listening, building trust, coaching under pressure, keeping people connected to a shared purpose) and frames them as capabilities most organisations leave to chance “or hoping generic training will solve (it rarely does).” Pitches this as the highest-leverage problem L&D and HR can solve right now.
Engagement
2 reactions · below the stage threshold but vendor-CEO post with a tight thesis that overlaps ours; comment positions Toby as the practitioner above the generic-training fix
Post age
~12m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The “generic training rarely solves it” line is the right diagnosis. What we’re seeing with clients is that the new manager doesn’t need more content on what a difficult conversation is — they need to have already had one in a context where the stakes felt real but the cost of getting it wrong didn’t. The promotion cycle keeps producing managers who first try the conversation in a live, high-stakes moment.

Run notes — 2026-05-05

  • Run yielded 8 candidates: 2 CHRO (Flor Farsadi, Lucia Tudor) · 1 ICP (Maame Agyarkwa) · 5 Influencer (Rachel Yates, Yolanda Pemberton, Olga Mukhina, Rachael Healey, Rupert Hillier).
  • Audience A was thin this run — most CHRO/HR posts surfacing in past-week were vendors or job ads. Flor Farsadi qualifies on practitioner identity (20-year ex-Unilever/Danone/Mitsubishi HR leader) plus original 42-interview EMEA HR Director research, even though she’s currently between roles. Lucia Tudor is a sitting Global HR Business Partner with a Path 2 manager-readiness reflection.
  • Audience B yielded one strong NFM/COCON practitioner (Maame Agyarkwa) anchored to the FCA 2024 wholesale-firm survey. Other compliance searches (three lines of defence, compliance theatre, Section 166, Consumer Duty year 2) were dominated by vendors or non-UK posts; nothing else cleared the bar.
  • Audience C was the strongest pull this run. Rachel Yates and Olga Mukhina are particularly close to Toby’s thesis and the comments can be additive without sounding piggy-back. Rachael Healey (RPC partner) flagged for Track-2 comment-mining on next run — FS compliance practitioner density likely high.
  • Searches that returned nothing usable: “Head of L&D” OR “Head of Learning” (mostly job ads and non-UK), “employee relations casework UK” (job ads only), “Head of Employee Relations” OR “HR Director” (vendor / open-to-work posts), “leadership programme impact OR stick” (off-topic UI/sustainability hits), “tick box compliance training” (cybersecurity vendors), “non-financial misconduct OR COCON” (COCON matched French “cocon” — refined to “NFM FCA”).
  • No login wall or automation issues encountered. Watchlist scan of seed accounts (Lewis Silkin, RPC, CIPD, Big 4) folded into keyword runs — Rachael Healey (RPC) was the only watchlist-direct hit this pass.
  • All slugs deterministic; engagement numbers low across the board this run (most posts <10 reactions) so most candidates are practitioner-quality not stage-quality. Toby’s discretion on which to engage.

2026-05-04-b — deeper pass: Crime and Policing Act 2026 wave (Royal Assent 29 April), watchlist law firms, ops manager voice for Audience A

1. Paulo Gomes

Operations & Business Operations Leader · Regulated Healthcare & Distribution · rare frontline-manager voice on absence pattern review, Bradford Factor scoring, and return-to-work conversations · "you can manage every absence and never manage absence" — classic activity-vs-outcomes framing on a manager scenario explicitly listed in Path 2
CHRO TODO
Role
Operations & Business Operations Leader · Regulated Healthcare & Distribution · warehouse manager population
Post topic
Walks through how he upgraded a warehouse return-to-work process from a series of one-by-one conversations into a pattern review: monthly instances by person, day-of-week mapping, Bradford Factor scores shared openly. The shift surfaced that Monday and Friday accounted for 55% of absences — opening a different conversation about shift allocation rather than individual blame. Closes with: "You can manage every absence and never manage absence." Real practitioner voice on a manager scenario the playbook explicitly targets.
Engagement
2 reactions · 1 comment · below the 25+ stage threshold but a genuine frontline-leader voice (rare for this run)
Post age
1d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
"Manage every absence and never manage absence" is the line. What we’re seeing with clients is that the return-to-work conversation is the moment where pattern data either becomes a fairer system conversation or becomes a confrontation the manager hasn’t been equipped for. Most haven’t actually rehearsed the version where the data points back at shift design rather than the individual.

2. Nick Henderson-Mayo

Strategic Leader in Governance & Risk · Global Regulatory Advisor · Head of Compliance · horizon-scan post on the King’s Speech 2026 (13 May) · explicit on the Crime and Policing Act expanding corporate criminal liability and senior manager exposure · published via VinciWorks (vendor) but the practitioner identity is real
ICP TODO
Role
Strategic Leader in Governance & Risk · Global Regulatory Advisor · Head of Compliance (VinciWorks)
Post topic
Looks ahead to the King’s Speech (13 May) and the legislative agenda for compliance teams. Lists what’s already landed (Employment Rights Act reshaping workplace compliance; Crime and Policing Act expanding corporate criminal liability and senior manager exposure) and what’s expected to come (Financial Services Bill, Equality (Race and Disability) Bill, plus Cyber Security and Resilience and Public Office (Accountability) Bills carrying over). Concise compliance-leader briefing.
Engagement
3 reactions · 1 repost
Post age
~1h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Useful map. The Crime and Policing Act piece is the one most compliance teams are still catching up to — the senior-manager exposure widening means the policy won’t do the work, the firm has to be able to show how it actually shapes day-to-day decisions. Worth war-gaming a few specific scenarios with the senior population before September lands on top.

3. Isabella Macfarlane ACII

Underwriter, turned Regulator, turned Consultant · Head of London Markets at Insurance Compliance Services Ltd · maps the legislative stack employers and FCA-authorised firms now face: Worker Protection Act, EHRC enforcement powers, Employment Rights Act 2025 NDA changes, and the September NFM extension · classic London-market regulatory voice

Role
Head of London Markets at Insurance Compliance Services Ltd · Underwriter, turned Regulator, turned Consultant

Reinforces a colleague (Casey Knight) post on the new FCA NFM rules with a wider stack: the Worker Protection Act preventative duty (Oct 2024), EHRC enforcement powers, Employment Rights Act 2025 alignment of harassment reporting and whistleblowing processes, NDAs and confidentiality clauses now restricted on sexual harassment disclosures, and void NDAs on witness restrictions. Practitioner-led summary — gives FS compliance leads in London markets the full picture.

quote-replied to Casey Knight (3 reactions on parent), low standalone engagement · useful authority signal for ICP follow
Post age
4d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Helpful stitching. What we’re seeing in London-market firms is the legislative stack lands on top of certified populations who’ve had the policy walk-through but rarely the lived rehearsal — particularly on third-party harassment scenarios where the new NDA position changes how the witness conversation has to be handled in the moment.

4. Julian Godfray

Counsel at Baker McKenzie, Compliance, Investigations and International Trade · clean explainer of the Crime and Policing Act 2026 (Royal Assent 29 April) widening corporate criminal liability via senior managers to ANY criminal offence · strongest watchlist post in the run, 32 reactions on a fresh statute · rich Track-2 mining ground
Influencer TODO
Role
Counsel at Baker McKenzie · Compliance, Investigations and International Trade
Post topic
Explains the Crime and Policing Act 2026 (CPA), Royal Assent 29 April, broadening corporate criminal liability for senior-manager conduct from specified economic crimes (under the Economic Crime and Corporate Transparency Act 2023) to ANY criminal offence. Expanded scope now includes modern slavery, environmental and regulatory breaches, plus economic crimes. Calls out reassessing risk exposure and ensuring senior managers are appropriately trained — "while the definition of ‘senior manager’ is going to be subject to debate, it will include individuals who play a significant role in decision-making or management."
Engagement
32 reactions · 1 comment · strong stage
Post age
2d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The training point is where this gets uncomfortable. "Senior manager" widening into anyone with a significant decision-making role, on any criminal offence — firms will need to be able to evidence that those people have actually been put through the conversations and judgement calls the new test contemplates. Reassessing risk exposure on paper isn’t the same as showing the behaviour holds up.

Partner, Contentious regulatory at Foot Anstey · recasts the identification doctrine reform under the Crime and Policing Act 2026 · "essential bank holiday reading for board members, GCs and compliance heads" · second strong watchlist law-firm post in the run on the same fresh trigger

Authority-signal post into the buying-committee audience for Audience B.

Companion piece to the Baker McKenzie note this week. The board / GC / compliance-head audience is the right one — though "essential reading" only does the work if it gets paired with a stress test of how the firm’s senior population would actually handle a live regulatory exposure under the widened doctrine. Most haven’t had that conversation yet.

6. Usman Hamid Malik

Associate Corporate (M&A / P.E.) at White & Case LLP · "UK corporate criminal liability reaches its peak: how corporates can respond to increasing exposure" article on the same Crime and Policing Act 2026 trigger · framed for corporate / private equity audience · third watchlist firm in the run on this fresh statute — notable convergence

Associate Corporate (M&A / P.E.) at White & Case LLP

Promotes a White & Case article on UK corporate criminal liability reaching its peak after the Crime and Policing Act 2026, framing the shift from the historic identification doctrine ("directing mind and will") to a senior-manager test. Aimed at corporate and PE audiences — useful add to the run because it shows the same statute being unpacked for a corporate-buyer (rather than purely FS) audience. Convergence of three watchlist firms in 48h is a meaningful signal.
