0 visible reactions · below threshold but watchlist firm + fresh trigger keeps it relevant
Post age
2h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The corporate / PE framing is the one most boards I work with are slowest to absorb — "directing mind and will" was always abstract enough that "we have a policy" felt like enough. Senior-manager test makes it personal and specific, which means the rehearsal of the conversations and judgement calls those individuals will be observed making becomes the actual evidence.

Run notes — 2026-05-04-b

  • Second pass after Toby asked for a deeper, more expansive run. Six additional candidates: 1 CHRO (finally), 2 Compliance ICP, 3 Influencer. Combined with run 1 (7 candidates), today closes at 13 total candidates.
  • Headline finding: The Crime and Policing Act 2026 (Royal Assent 29 April) is the dominant fresh trigger this week. Three watchlist law firms wrote substantive practitioner pieces on it within 48h: Baker McKenzie (Julian Godfray, 32 reactions), Foot Anstey (Sonya Zywko, partner-level), and White & Case (Usman Hamid Malik). The senior-manager-test widening to ANY criminal offence is the single piece of news that touches both Audience B (FS compliance) and Audience A (corporate boards / GC / risk) at the same time. Worth promoting "Crime and Policing Act" + senior-manager liability to a permanent search bank entry.
  • Audience A breakthrough — Paulo Gomes. First genuine frontline-manager voice in the day’s run. Operations leader in regulated healthcare distribution writing concretely about absence pattern review, Bradford Factor, and return-to-work conversations — exactly the manager scenario Path 2 targets. Engagement is light, but the practitioner-substance is the strongest CHRO/HR-adjacent post the day produced. A clear win versus run 1, where Audience A came back empty.
  • Watchlist deep-scan worked this time. Direct keyword searches with named firms returned material from Bates Wells (positive action workshop, low engagement, parked), Foot Anstey, White & Case, and Baker McKenzie. The pattern: legal news triggers + watchlist firm name + past-week date is more reliable than the generic "regulation name" search alone. Lewis Silkin still didn’t surface anything past-week — either a genuine quiet period or the platform suppressing repeat brand-name searches; will try named-employee searches next run (Bethan Carney, Rachel Heawood).
  • Path 3/4 expanded sweep returned little. "Immersive learning / conversation simulation" was vendor-heavy and off-vertical (cybersecurity gamification, VC programmes); "training fatigue / sheep dip" returned nothing usable; "manager as coach / leader as coach" the same; "deliberate practice + leader/manager" returned job ads. The intent-rich language is genuinely thin in any single seven-day window — it surfaces in bursts. Recommend rotating Path 3/4 keyword exposure to ~once every 4-5 days rather than every run.
  • Track-2 comment mining still pending. Did not get to opening the high-engagement posts (Ask for Angela UK at 53 reactions / 21 reposts; VinciWorks at 31 reactions / 13 reposts; Corina Dias at 32 reactions / 6 reposts) and reading the comment streams for ICP-matching practitioners. Those three posts together are the most productive Track-2 pond in the day’s scan and should be the first move of the next run.
  • Other useful but not-carded mentions. Brian Polk (EMEA FS regulation; April FinReg horizon-scan, 49 pages) is a useful watchlist add for monthly regulatory scans. Hilary Galea-Lauri (carded in run 1) is publishing a "Lessons from the Boardroom" series — track for Part 3+ in the coming weeks. Tuki Attuquayefio (PsychReady.ai, run 1 mention) is the closest peer-founder in the AI-roleplay-for-regulated-conversation lane — worth a profile-watch, not a carded engagement.
  • Convergence signal worth flagging: three watchlist law firms publishing on the same statute within 48h means the comment threads on those three posts are the single highest-yield Track-2 mining ground in the day’s data. Specifically Julian Godfray (32 reactions) is most likely to attract FS compliance heads in the comments — that’s the first thread to open next run.

2026-05-04 — quiet Audience A; strong Compliance ICP (NED + SMF + crypto/EMI), law-firm and coach influencer stages

1. Rhoda Kalejaiye

Head of Compliance & MLRO (SMF16 & SMF17) · FCA International Fintech Forum reflection · argues responsible innovation must be designed in as architecture, not bolted on · FCA leaning on Consumer Duty / SMCR / market integrity rather than writing new rulebooks for AI
ICP TODO
Role
Head of Compliance & MLRO (SMF16 & SMF17) · UK fintech
Post topic
Reflects on UK Fintech Week / FCA International Fintech Forum. Argues "the most important insights often lie in what isn't said." Points to ARCHITECTURE as the differentiator: effective firms embed regulation into products and decision-making from day one rather than treating it as overhead. Notes the FCA is leaning on established frameworks (Consumer Duty, SMCR, market integrity) rather than writing new rulebooks for AI — the gap is execution, not innovation.
Engagement
22 reactions · 4 comments · 1 repost
Post age
3d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The architecture point is the right one. Most firms I work with have the policies; what they don’t have is evidence that the people on the front line can actually do the behaviour the framework requires when the moment lands. That’s where the FCA’s outcomes-based supervision will start to bite.

2. Hilary Galea-Lauri

iNED · Audit & Risk Committee Chair · Trustee · ex-KPMG Partner · Insurance / Banking / Fintech · "Lessons from the Boardroom Part 2: why the Three Lines of Defence can give boards a false sense of security" · explicit NED-with-risk-oversight target from the spec
ICP TODO
Role
Independent Non-Executive Director · Audit & Risk Committee Chair · Trustee · Former Partner KPMG · Insurance / Banking / Fintech
Post topic
Argues the three lines of defence model "fails when boards receive it passively." First line stretched, second line under-resourced, third line provides assurance "over what was" — and the board sits above receiving filtered, framed, sometimes softened reporting. The model only works "when each line is genuinely independent, adequately resourced, and when the board refuses to treat the model as a passive comfort." Direct alignment with Toby’s evidence-vs-paperwork thesis.
Engagement
13 reactions · 1 comment
Post age
2d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
"Reading the silences as carefully as the submissions" is the part most boards skip. What we’re seeing with clients is that the second-line MI tells you everything is green right up until a regulator interview where someone in the first line explains, on tape, that they were never resourced or trained for the conversation they’ve just had to handle. The model works when the board is willing to test the behaviour, not just review the slide.

3. Omasirichukwu Anyanwu

Global Head of Compliance & MLRO · Co-Founder ChatKYC · UK fintech / digital assets / payments · mapping the EMI safeguarding (PS25/12, 7 May) and crypto FSMA (30 Sept) compliance calendar · practical "operationalise before the deadline, not against it" stance
ICP TODO
Role
Global Head of Compliance & MLRO · Co-Founder ChatKYC · SMF role · Fintechs & Digital Asset Firms
Post topic
Maps out the next 18 months of UK regulatory load for firms touching both payments and crypto: PS25/12 EMI safeguarding rules in force 7 May (CASS-style daily recs, monthly FCA returns, statutory trust letters, resolution packs, annual audits), then crypto FSMA licensing applications open 30 Sept with go-live Oct 2027. Practitioner advice: operationalise the workflow, controls and documentation before the deadline; stay close to FCA advisories; SMFs should keep an open line with the regulator.
Engagement
11 reactions · 4 comments · 1 repost
Post age
4d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The "build the workflow before the deadline, not against it" point is exactly right. What we’re seeing with clients is that the firms who get caught aren’t the ones who missed a date — they’re the ones who hit the date with a policy nobody on the front line has ever practised under pressure. PS25/12 in particular will expose that.

The third-party piece is where most employers I work with are weakest — the policy is in place, the manager training has happened, but very few have actually rehearsed how a frontline lead responds to a client or contractor crossing the line in the moment. That’s the gap the EHRC will probe.

The firms I work with are finding the gap is rarely in the rulebook — it’s in whether their certified population can be observed handling a live NFM scenario the way COCON 1.1.7FR now expects.

6. Paul Emslie

Founder, My Coaching Place · Ex C-Suite turned Executive Coach · explicit "training but behaviour doesn’t stick / capability gap with tools that weren’t designed for scale" framing · named Wickes (Claire Bailey), HPE, SAS, Social Work England as clients · classic L&D-buyer stage

Role
Founder, My Coaching Place · Ex C-Suite turned Executive Coach · 1,200+ leaders developed
Post topic
"Most organisations say they’re developing their leaders. Very few are doing it in a way that actually changes behaviour at scale." Walks through the standard pattern — promote high performers, offer training, behaviour doesn’t stick — and reframes it as a design problem, not a motivation problem. Pitches scaled 1:1 coaching as the answer; references Wickes (Claire Bailey), HPE, SAS, Social Work England. Comment thread is L&D buyers.
Engagement
21 reactions · 22 comments · productive stage
Post age
3d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The "design problem, not a motivation problem" framing is right. What we’re seeing with clients is the behaviour rarely transfers because the system never gives leaders a low-stakes place to practise the new behaviour under pressure before the real moment lands. Coaching solves part of that; structured rehearsal solves the other part.

7. Tom Hine

Lawyer & financial markets expert · long-form board-training writeup on the interaction of section 172 of the Companies Act 2006 with FCA expectations (Threshold Conditions, Principle 11, Consumer Duty, SMCR) · substantive piece even at modest engagement · useful Track-2 stage for FS NEDs and General Counsel
Influencer TODO
Role
Lawyer & financial markets expert · delivers regulated-firm board training
Post topic
Recap of director training on the friction between section 172 (act in good faith to promote the success of the company for the benefit of its members) and FCA expectations (Threshold Conditions, Principle 11, Consumer Duty, SMF Conduct Rules). Argues "in a regulated firm, ‘success’ cannot coherently exclude regulatory standing." Two practical points: section 172 analysis should appear in board papers and minutes whenever a decision has a regulatory dimension; directors should be slow to assume section 172 protects them if they side with shareholder over regulator in the short term.
Engagement
8 reactions · 2 comments · 1 repost · below the 25+ stage threshold but unusually substantive for the comment audience
Post age
21h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The "should appear in board papers and minutes" point is the one most boards under-do. What we’re seeing in clients is that the section 172 limbs — long-term consequences, high standards of business conduct — only really get tested when a regulator asks the board to demonstrate how they weighed them in a specific decision. The discipline is in the record, not the framework.

Run notes — 2026-05-04

  • Seven candidates: zero CHRO, three Compliance ICP, four Influencer. Quality bar held over volume — CHRO bucket genuinely thin in this scan, so skipped rather than padded.
  • Audience A (CHRO) was the weakest segment of the day. Searches across Path 1 (workplace investigation, ER casework, EHRC harassment, Worker Protection Act + reasonable steps), Path 2 (knowing-doing gap, difficult conversations, first-time / accidental manager, leadership development doesn’t stick, manager capability), Path 3 (AI roleplay) and Path 4 (compliance theatre, tick-box training, mandatory training doesn’t work, compliance training broken) returned mostly: (a) job ads, (b) overseas content (South Africa, Australia, Malta, Ireland, Asia, MENA), (c) vendors / coaches at the wrong scale, or (d) very small consultancies. Notable near-misses parked: Pink Fluff HR Consultancy (UK, but tiny scale and no audience), Alan Gauci (Malta fractional HR, good "management trap" framing but not UK), Yolanda Pemberton (vendor C territory — NWAF Framework founder), Darnecia Pierce ("Develop the leader, yes. But redesign the architecture." — only 4 reactions).
  • Strongest find: Hilary Galea-Lauri — iNED + Audit & Risk Committee Chair + ex-KPMG Partner explicitly arguing in public that the Three Lines of Defence gives boards a false sense of security. Exactly the NED-with-risk-oversight target the spec calls out, and she’s writing the Real Talk thesis for us. Worth tracking her "Lessons from the Boardroom" series for Part 3+ in coming weeks.
  • Compliance ICP was the strongest segment. Three SMF / MLRO holders posting substantive practitioner content within the past 4 days: Rhoda Kalejaiye (architecture / FCA Fintech Forum), Hilary Galea-Lauri (3LoD critique), Omasirichukwu Anyanwu (PS25/12 + crypto FSMA timeline). All three are credible voices, all three have explicit alignment with Toby’s positioning.
  • Audience C: four solid stages. Corina Dias (Sintons LLP / CIPD Tees Valley) is the highest-engagement post in the run at 32 reactions, 6 reposts, and the named-attendees list is a Track-2 fishing pond for north-east UK HR practitioners. Alan Ward (Stephenson Harwood) and Paul Emslie (My Coaching Place / Wickes / HPE / SAS) round out the quality stages; Tom Hine is below the 25+ engagement bar but the substance is unusually strong.
  • Path 4 ("compliance theatre") is now well-trodden — Marina Kilcline (1 May) was the strongest entry, and the past-week sweep returned mostly Khushi Chouhan (cybersecurity, off-vertical), Maison Gildæ (Australia / off-vertical) and old reposts. Likely worth resting that exact phrase for 2-3 days and rotating to "paper compliance" / "policy on paper vs in practice" instead.
  • Path 3 (AI roleplay / simulation) returned Tuki Attuquayefio (PsychReady.ai co-founder, Western Sydney) — same conceptual lane as Real Talk but for psychologist training, not L&D for managers. Engagement too low to warrant a card today, but worth a profile-watch — if his network includes UK clinical-leadership L&D buyers, he’s a kindred spirit and a potential category collaborator.
  • Watchlist scan of Lewis Silkin company page returned no readable feed text via get_page_text; keyword search for "Lewis Silkin" + Worker Protection past-week returned no results. Worth investigating whether the company-page route still works in this run environment, and falling back to named-employee searches (Bethan Carney, Rachel Heawood, etc.) on next run.
  • FCA published PS26/6 last week (SMCR streamlining, Phase 1 changes effective 24 April). Practitioner takes are starting to appear — Pregeshni Maduramuthu (Arbor Law) was the best one, but she was already on the 1 May card. Worth a fresh sweep mid-week as more compliance leads write up their interpretations.

2026-05-01-b — second pass: compliance-theatre, corporate liability widening, watchlist law-firm

1. Marina Kilcline

Fractional / Interim Chief People Officer · explicit "compliance theatre" framing applied to AI adoption pressure on CFOs and leaders · sits exactly in Real Talk's narrative lane

Fractional and Interim Chief People Officer · Multi-Award Winning Executive Coach · Mentor / Board Advisor / NED
Post topic
Argues there's a new anxiety in organisations — not fear of AI replacing people, but pressure to use AI visibly. Says when adoption is pushed onto an exhausted system you don't get transformation, you get "compliance theatre — people opening tools they don't use, adding AI-generated slides to decks nobody reads, ticking a box that says yes, we're doing the thing." Direct alignment with Toby's activity-vs-outcomes thesis.

2 reactions · below the 25+ stage threshold but the framing is unusually well-aligned and her network is fractional CPOs / NEDs — exactly Toby's economic buyer cluster
Post age
~1h
Link
Find on LinkedIn

"Compliance theatre wearing a tech hat" lands. What we're seeing with clients is the same dynamic with leadership and compliance training generally — the pressure is to roll something out, not to know whether managers can actually do the behaviour when it matters. Doing less, better is the right call.

2. Murray Grainger

Governance, Risk & Compliance practitioner · Crime and Policing Act 2026 widening of UK corporate criminal liability to senior managers · argues whistleblowing systems must be tested in practice, not on paper · classic Audience B narrative
ICP TODO
Role
Governance, Risk & Compliance | Business Ethics & Integrity | Collaborative Partnerships | IESE GEMBA · independent practitioner profile
Post topic
Crime and Policing Act 2026 broadens corporate criminal liability beyond economic crime to any criminal offence committed by a senior manager. Argues "paper compliance programmes aren't enough" — the question is whether speak-up channels actually work when the wrongdoer is the decision-maker. Lists five tests: independent channels, leadership tone, prompt and fair investigation, zero tolerance enforced, regular testing.
Engagement
Low directly visible · topic is fresh and well-framed
Post age
~24m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
"If a senior leader is acting unlawfully today, would we know about it?" is the question every board should be asking but few are stress-testing properly. What we're seeing with clients is that speak-up channels look fine on paper and fall apart at the manager-conversation layer — the moment between someone raising a concern and the manager deciding what to do with it. That's the bit that needs rehearsing, not the policy.

3. Paula Jefferson

Partner at Clyde & Co · safeguarding / abuse claims / redress specialist · Crime and Policing Act 2026 changes to limitation periods for childhood sexual abuse claims · watchlist law firm; safeguarding adjacent ICP angle

Sharing colleague Sharon Moohan's overview of how the Crime and Policing Act 2026 (now in royal assent) changes limitation rules for claimants sexually abused under age 18. Significant for safeguarding-exposed sectors — social housing, healthcare, social care, education, sport. Useful follow for Audience C and a comment-mining stage for safeguarding leads in Audiences A and B.

Post age
~1h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The limitation reform will land hardest on organisations whose safeguarding records were never set up to evidence "all reasonable steps" historically. Most safeguarding leads I work with say the gap isn't policy — it's whether frontline managers actually felt confident to act on early signals at the time. That capability question is going to come back into focus as more cases extend back through the timeline.

Run notes — 2026-05-01-b

  • Second pass run after the first one finished — targeted at clusters that didn't yield much in pass 1: watchlist law firms (Lewis Silkin, Dentons, Eversheds, Pinsent Masons, Linklaters, Slaughter and May, Hogan Lovells, Browne Jacobson, Bates Wells, Clyde & Co, DLA Piper), CIPD / Personnel Today / HR Magazine, Path 3 (deliberate practice / rehearsal / practice platform / safe to practise), Path 4 dissatisfaction (compliance theatre / paper compliance), and Tier 1 industry checks (NHS safeguarding, social housing, Crime and Policing Act 2026).
  • Three additive candidates — smaller batch by design. Quality bar held: no thin entries from a search that returned mostly noise.
  • Strongest find: Marina Kilcline — the post explicitly uses "compliance theatre" framing on AI adoption, which is unusually close to Toby's positioning. Her network of fractional CPOs / NEDs is the Cowork-economic-buyer cluster.
  • Murray Grainger: timely Crime and Policing Act 2026 take with five concrete tests for whether a whistleblowing system actually works. Independent GRC voice, classic Audience B fit even though direct engagement is light.
  • Paula Jefferson (Clyde & Co): low like count but the comment thread on a watchlist-law-firm post about a fresh statute is a productive Track-2 fishing pond — safeguarding leads in social housing / healthcare / sport will surface there over the next few days.
  • Crime and Policing Act 2026 (royal assent this week) is a fresh trigger that didn't appear in any pass-1 candidate — worth promoting it to its own keyword search ("Crime and Policing Act 2026") in the next 7-10 day rotation, alongside section 14 / senior manager liability.
  • May Day noise still present even on second pass — "HRD harassment", "social housing safeguarding", and "Mercer / Korn Ferry / BCG + CHRO" all returned mostly Workers' Day or unrelated content. Re-run those clusters mid-week.
  • Watchlist scan still pending — carrying forward to next run as a first-pass priority.

Curious what Mark's measurement approach looked like.

Year 2 boards seem to be sharpening up on the difference. Good luck with the rest of the build.

4. John Hinchliffe

Head of Digital Learning at Emirates NBD · "AI is the tractor and we've become farmers" · LT26 follow-on reflection post · high-traction L&D thought-leader voice
Influencer TODO
Role
Multi Award-Winning Head of Digital Learning, Emirates NBD · named one of the Top 30 Trailblazing Thought Leaders in eLearning
Post topic
Long, thoughtful Learning Technologies recap arguing AI is the tractor — speeds up production, but doesn't understand the soil (culture, politics, manager pressure, why people behave the way they do). Argues L&D's value is increasingly knowing when a "learning request" is actually a performance / leadership / confidence problem. Direct alignment with Real Talk's positioning.
Engagement
23+ reactions · 2 comments · just under the 25+ stage threshold but climbing
Post age
~30m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The tractor analogy lands. What we're seeing with clients is that AI is great at planting more content faster — but the bottleneck has never really been content volume. It's whether managers can actually have the conversation when it matters. The soil work is mostly about giving people somewhere safe to practise the behaviour, not somewhere to consume more of the theory.

5. Pregeshni Maduramuthu

Partner | Head of Managed Services at Arbor Law · PS26/6 SMCR Phase 1 breakdown with key dates · classic Audience C "stage" for compliance practitioners
Influencer TODO
Role
Partner | Head of Managed Services | Financial Services | GRC | Outsourced Compliance Services, Arbor Law
Post topic
Practitioner-flavoured breakdown of FCA PS26/6 with key Phase 1 dates (24 Apr live, 10 Jul reporting changes, 1 Sept NFM alignment). Frames Phase 1 as removing operational friction and flags that Phase 2 will determine if SMCR is actually simpler. Pulls compliance / SMCR practitioners into the comments — good Track-2 mining ground.
Engagement
Low directly on this post but high relevance · reposts FCA's own announcement (5 reactions, 1 repost on the underlying)
Post age
1h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Useful breakdown. The September NFM alignment is the bit most firms I work with are underestimating — once COCON 1.1.7FR lands, the question stops being "do we have a policy" and becomes "can we evidence that managers handle this competently in the moment." Phase 1 is mostly process; Phase 2 is mostly capability.

6. David Armes

Independent commercial-capability designer · sharp post on why training events don't drive behaviour change · learning transfer / behaviour-change vocabulary · squarely in Real Talk's narrative lane
Influencer TODO
Role
"Helping people to discover they can find growth, and enjoy getting it" · commercial-capability programme designer (independent / consultancy)
Post topic
Argues organisations don't become more commercial when people "complete modules" — behaviour shifts in live moments. Lists the three blockers: training treats behaviour like a light switch, the system pulls people back to old habits, managers reinforce intent not action. Calls for L&D to stop asking "did they like the program" and start asking "what is now easier, safer, or expected to be done differently." Direct knowing-doing-gap framing.
Engagement
Low directly but high topical alignment · "Only connections can comment" suggests selective audience
Post age
~18m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
"Managers reinforce intent, not action" — that's exactly the bit most leadership programmes miss. The shift we're seeing is from measuring whether people enjoyed the workshop to measuring whether they can actually do the behaviour under realistic pressure. The system pull-back is real; it's why one-off training rarely sticks without a way to keep practising in context.

Run notes — 2026-05-01

  • Heavy noise across UK searches today: International Workers' Day (May 1) flooded most "employer / employee / labour / harassment" feeds with non-UK celebratory content. Path 1 regulation searches (Worker Protection Act, NFM, Employment Rights Act) skewed especially heavily to vendor / law firm posts.
  • Strongest pick of the run: Jennifer Lawrence's B&M Retail appointment. New CHRO arrival is the textbook buying trigger and Toby's outreach window is now — before her first 90-day plan is set.
  • Stacey Moorman is a small-firm ICP candidate but the Customer Assurance Manager role at a UK building society in the middle of Consumer Duty board prep is genuinely the right ICP — FCA-regulated, customer-outcomes adjacent, in the buying moment. Worth the engagement even though The Cambridge BS is below the 500+ ceiling we usually look for.
  • John Hinchliffe's "AI is the tractor" post is just under the 25-reaction influencer threshold (sitting at 23 at capture) but climbing fast and very on-message — included anyway as the run's strongest stage. Emirates NBD geography is non-UK; treating it as a thought-leader stage rather than a lead.
  • Eleanor Rowland (Fulham FC) is an edge case — Fulham is a small employer but the Director of Safeguarding & Inclusion role is exactly the Path 1 champion profile, and the Business in the Community / Lewis Silkin event circle is a useful one for Toby. Including with the caveat noted on the card.
  • Pregeshni Maduramuthu (Arbor Law) and David Armes are included as influencer "stages" rather than viral posts — engagement was low directly on each, but the topical alignment with Toby's narrative lane is unusually strong. Worth a comment for visibility in the right rooms.
  • Could not retrieve direct post URLs — LinkedIn search results pages don't expose post anchors in the HTML accessibility tree, and the javascript_tool path is blocked on chrome-extension URLs as expected. All links are people-search URLs per the skill's default; Toby clicks through to the person and locates the post from their feed.
  • Searches run this session: knowing-doing gap, reasonable steps harassment employer, tick box compliance training, Worker Protection Act + all reasonable steps, NFM/COCON, Consumer Duty + vulnerable customers, AI roleplay learning, difficult conversations manager, compliance training doesn't work, section 166, compliance culture, employee relations + casework/harassment, line manager capability, Employment Rights Act + HR, mandatory training doesn't work, performance conversation / underperformance manager, three lines of defence compliance, Head of Compliance FCA, first time / new manager, FCA + NFM, leadership development programmes, Lewis Silkin / Eversheds + harassment, speak up culture, Head of L&D / Head of Learning UK, Consumer Duty year 2/3, CIPD research, Head of Employee Relations / ER Manager, EHRC harassment, Pinsent Masons / Linklaters / Slaughter and May + harassment, People Director / CPO + capability, learning transfer / behaviour change, Worker Protection Act + Head of HR, Acas + harassment / grievance.
  • No watchlist scan completed this run — budget consumed by keyword sweep through the May Day noise. Add to next run's first pass.

2026-04-30 — LT26 day-2 sweep + compliance & HR practitioner scan

1. Quym Greaves Assoc CIPD

OD Specialist at NHS Arden & GEM CSU · published research on NHS talent management practices · explicit link between manager unprep, sickness absence and grievance escalation · Tier 1 healthcare ICP
CHRO TODO
Role
OD Specialist at NHS Arden & Greater East Midlands Commissioning Support Unit (NHS shared service body supporting ICBs and trusts). Conducted the research as part of a Senior People Professional Degree Apprenticeship at the University of Exeter. Audience A — in-house NHS people-function practitioner.
Post topic
Blog post sharing research findings on strengthening talent management practice in publicly funded systems. Frames manager capability as a financial and human-cost lever: when managers feel unprepared, sickness absence rises; when processes are poorly executed, grievances escalate; when staff don’t feel safe to share concerns, innovation is impeded. Explicitly couples manager capability with psychological safety as the lever for reducing avoidable escalation.
Engagement
18 reactions · 1 comment · 0 reposts
Post age
6d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The line about unprepared managers driving sickness absence and grievance escalation lands hard — that’s exactly the cost most workforce plans don’t price in. What we’re seeing with NHS clients is the gap rarely sits at the policy or framework level; it sits in whether line managers can actually hold the harder conversations under pressure. Would be interested to read the full piece.

2. Mazhar Manzoor

Head of Compliance & Risk · CASS, MLRO experience across asset managers, CFDs, banks · detailed practitioner breakdown of the SMCR reform timeline (24 April / 10 July / 1 September alignment)

Head of Compliance & Risk, CASS PRz, MLRO — in-house regulatory leader with hands-on Op Resilience, Consumer Duty, PSD2, IFPR, SMCR, MIFID experience. Audience B in-scope role (SMF16-equivalent) — speaks the “F&P aligned with annual appraisals” / “SMF7 / SMF18 / SoR” vocabulary that signals real practitioner.
Post topic
Plain-text breakdown of the latest SMCR reform package: HM Treasury / FCA / PRA changes from 24 April (12-week rule, F&P, SMF7/18 guidance, criminal record check validity), 10 July (overlapping SMF removal, enhanced firm thresholds), and 1 September (alignment with expanded NFM rules). Reads as a sense-making note for peers, not a sales pitch.
Engagement
8 reactions · 0 comments · 0 reposts
Post age
1d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Useful breakdown — the September NFM alignment is the bit most firms I work with are still under-cooking. They’ve mapped the rule changes into policy and training, but the question on the table is whether their managers can actually run a conduct conversation that holds up if it ends up in front of a panel. Activity-evidence is one thing; behavioural evidence is what regulators are starting to push on.

3. Giovanni Giamminola

Just keynoted Learning Technologies 26 in front of ~1,500 L&D directors · “we’ve all just been promoted” · cognitive governance over AI agents in the L&D function · named-watchlist style amplifier
Influencer TODO
Role
AI Transformation & Governance for CEOs and Boards · Affiliated Fellow, UCL Centre for Sustainable Business. Influencer / advisor — Audience C amplifier; keynote stage gives this post outsized reach into UK L&D leadership today.
Post topic
Reflection on his LT26 keynote: AI absorbing the administrative gravity of HR/L&D work, freeing leaders back to relational and judgement-led work. Frames it as a “promotion, not a threat” and introduces “Cognitive Governance” as the discipline of holding authority while delegating execution to AI agents. Tags Donald H Taylor — expect heavy L&D-leader comment activity in the next 24h.
Engagement
2 reactions · 0 comments · 0 reposts (just posted; will accelerate fast)
Post age
~17m at scan time
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The cognitive-governance framing is the right one — the firms moving fastest aren’t the ones with the most agents, they’re the ones whose leaders can still defend the decisions the agents are making. Most of the L&D teams I work with are realising the freed-up time has to go into building the human capability that AI can’t do for them — the conversations under pressure, the judgement calls. The hard part is whether they’ve actually rehearsed it.

4. Dr. Stryker Calvez

I/O psychology lens on why “respectful workplace” policies don’t prevent harm · explicit framing that policies manage liability but don’t moderate behaviour in real time · right inside Real Talk’s narrative
Influencer TODO
Role
Specialist in the strategic enablement of reconciliation, equity, diversity, inclusion and belonging. Independent thought-leader / consultant — Audience C influencer voice that practitioners already follow.
Post topic
Argument that most workplace harm doesn’t happen because people haven’t read the policy — they’ve clicked “I acknowledge” and could probably pass the quiz. Calls out the gap between policy as liability management and policy as behaviour control, and points to everyday leadership behaviours (role clarity, fairness, psychological safety, how feedback is given) as the actual harm-prevention lever. Lands the “guardrails not force fields” line that practitioners will quote.
Engagement
4 reactions · 1 comment · 0 reposts
Post age
9h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The “policies are good at managing liability but much less effective at moderating behaviour in real time” line is the whole problem most clients are grappling with. The shift that actually moves the dial is treating people-management as real work for supervisors — not an interruption — and giving them somewhere to actually rehearse the harder calls before the moment arrives. Policies tell you what to do; capability is whether you can do it under pressure.

5. Compliance Compass UK

Section 166 / Skilled Person review preparation toolkit · explicit framing that the skilled person assesses whether policies operate “in practice”, not on paper · classic Audience B comment-mining stage
Influencer TODO

UK compliance training / consultancy brand — vendor, but the kind that draws active Heads of Compliance and Compliance Officers into the comments. Audience C amplifier; Track-2 mining ground for Audience B.
Post topic
Practitioner playbook on the first 48 hours after receiving a Section 166 notice — common mistakes (delayed response, unprepared interviews, slow document production) and a structured toolkit. Key narrative line: the skilled person will form a view on the firm’s compliance culture by interviewing staff and testing whether policies work in practice. Direct overlap with Real Talk’s “evidence behaviour, not paperwork” story.
Engagement
1 reaction · 0 comments · 0 reposts (early; below the 25-reaction threshold but the topic is on-tone)
Post age
1d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The line about the skilled person forming a view on culture by interviewing staff is the bit most firms I work with under-prepare for. They’ll have the document set in good shape but haven’t actually rehearsed how their managers and front-line staff sound when they’re asked the harder “what do you do when…” questions cold. Stress-testing the people layer is where most of the real risk lives.

6. Keith Anderson

“Three rooms where every AI rollout gets decided. L&D is usually in the wrong one.” · behaviour-change angle on AI adoption · ex-Google / Meta / Uber / DoorDash · sits adjacent to LT26 conversation
Influencer TODO
Role
Behaviour-change advisor for CTOs on AI adoption (“The Activation Architecture”) · ex-Google, Meta, Uber, DoorDash. Influencer voice in the L&D + AI overlap that Real Talk’s ICP is paying attention to right now.
Post topic
Argument that AI rollouts get decided in three rooms — selection (CTO + procurement + vendor), deployment (IT + security + PM), adoption (L&D) — and L&D usually arrives in room three, six months too late, with a mandate but no budget. Frames the difference between “buying licenses” and “building capability”. Adjacent to Real Talk’s narrative about training that doesn’t transfer.
Engagement
6 reactions · 1 comment · 0 reposts
Post age
17h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The three-rooms framing is exactly right — and it generalises beyond AI tooling. We see the same pattern with regulatory programmes: legal and risk shape the policy, procurement signs the training vendor, and L&D inherits an enablement brief six months in. By the time anyone notices managers can’t actually run the conversation the policy assumes, the budget’s already spent on licenses.

Run notes — 2026-04-30

  • Output: 6 candidates (1 CHRO · 1 ICP · 4 Influencer). Lighter on Audience A than the ~3-per-bucket target — in-house HR / ER / L&D heads were quiet on UK-specific practitioner language this run; Path 1 clusters returned mostly Australian / South African or vendor content.
  • Best Audience A find: Quym Greaves at NHS Arden & GEM CSU — Tier 1 healthcare, named individual, research-based post explicitly tying manager unprep to sickness absence and grievance escalation.
  • Best Audience B find: Mazhar Manzoor (Head of Compliance & Risk) — only in-house compliance practitioner posting under their own name with substantive SMCR reform commentary in the past 24h. Note: also surfaced Compliance Compass UK’s S166 toolkit as Audience C comment-mining ground.
  • LT26 (Learning Technologies 2026, ExCeL London — runs today) is a major signal source: Giovanni Giamminola’s post-keynote reflection on “cognitive governance” is the strongest influencer-stage of the run. Watchlist hits in feed: Animaker, Access Learning (Gemma Siddons / Alan Hiddleston), Ross Dickie, Rebecca Hitchman (already logged).
  • Path 4 (status-quo challenge) keyword cluster “leadership development didn’t stick” returned no results in past week — likely poor phrasing; rotate to “workshop didn’t transfer” / “back to old habits” next run.
  • Skipped duplicates already in last 7 days: Patrick McGovern (S&W Consumer Duty Y2), Moses Soussis (Consumer Duty ops redesign), Pathlight Associates (FCA Lot D outcomes).
  • Dr. Stryker Calvez post borderline on UK applicability (ROI sounds Canadian) but content is unambiguously Real-Talk territory and the comment can be offered without geography assumptions.
  • Twinvoice (AI roleplay competitor) and EBB, LLC (manager-development knowing-doing-gap content) seen but not logged — competitor space; adding to future-watch rather than commenting.
  • No comments posted — tracker entries only.

3. Patrick McGovern

Manager · Regulatory Consulting at S&W · FCA Consumer Duty Year 2 board-report findings · what Boards will need to evidence in Year 3
ICP TODO
Role
Manager, Regulatory Consulting at S&W (Smith & Williamson / Evelyn Partners successor advisory). UK FS regulatory consultant, sits on the Audience B ↔ C boundary (vendor / consultant by day-job, but the post is exactly the practitioner-language Heads of Compliance and Heads of Customer Outcomes are reading right now). Bridge profile — useful both as an Audience C influencer stage AND as a way to surface ICP commenters.
Post topic
FCA’s latest review of Consumer Duty Year 2 board reports: governance and use of data have improved, but the bar is moving higher. Six-page slide carousel covering: (1) what the FCA says has improved since Year 1, (2) where firms are still falling short, (3) what Boards will be expected to evidence in Year 3. Direct match for Real Talk’s “evidence outcomes, not process” framing.
Engagement
1 reaction · 0 comments · 0 reposts
Post age
~12h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The Year 2 to Year 3 jump is the one that exposes most of the gap. Year 1 was “is the framework in?”, Year 2 was “is the data flowing?”, Year 3 is “can the Board credibly say outcomes have changed?” What we’re seeing with clients is the MI is in fine shape but the underlying customer-conversation evidence is still thin — you can describe the shape of vulnerable-customer treatment but not always evidence it call by call.

The hardest part of “where are we now” on NFM is that most firms could pass a policy review and still fail the September test. Looking forward to the hybrid-working piece.

5. Nic Elliott

HR Most Influential 2025 · #1 Legal LinkedInfluencer Indies · five things employers need to think about ahead of the 2027 unfair-dismissal changes · 21 reactions inside 1h

HR/employment-law influencer (HRD Talks Podcast host, ELEVATE Expert). Re-engaging on a different angle is fine and his audience — HR leaders and people managers in mid-market UK firms — is exactly the room Toby wants to be visible in.
Post topic
Five things employers need to think about ahead of the unfair-dismissal changes coming in 2027 (protection at 6 months, no compensation cap). Practical action list: improve recruitment, onboard well, review probation length, train managers on performance, exit underperformers before end of 2026. Item 4 (manager training on performance) is the direct Real Talk hook.
Engagement
21 reactions · 2 comments · 0 reposts
Post age
~1h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Point 4 is the one most firms will quietly under-invest in until it’s too late. “Train managers to manage performance well” is sitting in every L&D plan already — the question is whether they can actually run the conversation when the tribunal clock is six months not two years. Most current programmes test knowledge, not behaviour under pressure.

6. Pathlight Associates

FCA/PRA skilled-person panel · Lot D Conduct of Business takeaways · “outcomes test, not a policy one” · NFM as behavioural indicator for market abuse and conflicts
Influencer TODO
Role
UK regulatory consultancy on the FCA/PRA skilled-person panel. Audience C company page (not personal). Useful stage because skilled-person commentary is read by Heads of Compliance and 2nd-line risk leads at SMCR firms — exactly Real Talk’s ICP. Tee-up post for Track-2 mining (any Heads of Compliance commenting are direct Audience B).
Post topic
Three takeaways from Lot D (Conduct of Business) of the FCA/PRA skilled-person panel: (1) Consumer Duty has shifted from “framework in place” to “evidence good outcomes” — price & value, consumer understanding, customer support; (2) vulnerability and inclusion — deep-dive reviews into how firms identify, monitor and treat in practice; (3) wholesale conduct — NFM as a behavioural indicator for market abuse and conflicts risks. Closes with the line: “for senior leaders, this is an outcomes test, not a policy one.”
Engagement
0 visible reactions · 0 comments · 0 reposts
Post age
~16h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The line that lands hardest here is “outcomes test, not a policy one.” Most firms can show a tidy policy stack against all three Lot D themes — very few can show, in evidence, that the conversations on the front line have actually changed. The NFM-as-behavioural-indicator framing is also a useful re-cast for compliance teams who are still treating it as a separate workstream from market-abuse surveillance.

7. Yvette Butterworth

Director Healthcare at WTW (Willis Towers Watson) · Employment Rights Act — employers shifting from compliance to preventative, joined-up workforce-sustainability approach
Influencer TODO
Role
Director Healthcare at WTW (Willis Towers Watson UK), advising employers on absence, wellbeing and workforce support. Big-4-adjacent consultancy — sits on the Audience C watchlist. Healthcare practice angle bridges into Tier 1 ICP industry.
Post topic
Reflection on client conversations: employers are moving past pure compliance with Employment Rights Act changes and looking at the wider operational impact — absence, wellbeing, workforce support. Sees a clear shift to preventative, joined-up approaches that integrate HR, line managers, wellbeing and operations. Frames this as a “workforce sustainability issue, not simply a policy update.” Asks how others are approaching it — explicitly designed to invite peer comment.
Engagement
0 visible reactions · 0 comments · 0 reposts
Post age
~41m
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The shift to “workforce sustainability rather than policy update” is the right re-frame — but it only works if line managers actually have the capability to run the early-intervention conversation. Most ER cases I see escalate not because policy is unclear but because the manager couldn’t or wouldn’t have the difficult conversation at week two. Preventative is a conversation question before it’s a policy one.

Run notes — 2026-04-29

  • Heavy vendor-skew across the regulation-name search clusters this run (Worker Protection Act, NFM-keyword, FCA enforcement). Practitioner posts genuinely thin in mid-week feed.

  • Audience B Track 1 returned only consultant / vendor side voices (S&W, Pathlight, Dectech) — no fresh in-house Heads of Compliance posting under their own name. Patrick McGovern routed as ICP because the post content is squarely on the buyer’s reading list and likely to surface practitioner commenters.
  • Strongest pick of the run: Nic Elliott — 21 reactions in the first hour, list-format post on the 2027 unfair-dismissal change with manager training as item 4 (direct Real Talk hook).
  • Watchlist hit: Dentons UK (Emma Carter) — new NFM article series, follow the next instalments on hybrid working.
  • Skipped duplicates already in last 7 days: Chris Syder (franchise/Worker Protection), Julie Ampadu (NFM event), Nic Elliott’s earlier Employment Rights Act 2026 post.

Low engagement (1 reaction) but the position — HR + Finance + IT modernisation, AI angle — sits adjacent to the buyer conversation Real Talk runs around AI-driven readiness rather than systems-driven HR data.

1 reaction · 0 comments · 0 reposts
Post age
~5h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Have a good two days. The interesting line at HR Tech this year is the gap between systems-of-record AI (better HR data) and systems-of-readiness AI (managers practising the conversation before the moment lands). Most stands will be on the first half of that line. Would be curious whose approach you think is actually closing the second half.

Run notes — plain LT26 + HR Tech sweep

  • 3 new cards added. 2 LT26 attendees (Mark Mccartney, Rebecca Hitchman) and 1 HR Tech attendee (Francis Smiley). All bucketed CHRO / Influencer; none are confirmed in-house buyers, all are consultant-side or coach-side voices attending as guests.
  • Plain “Learning Technologies” search (Toby’s explicit ask): confirmed the modifier-search bias call. The four-name shortlist from the morning’s targeted sweep (Michael Coates, Georgia Hannam, Genevieve Osborne, Serena Bugden) was already the right one — the only genuinely-new attendee voice the plain search added was Rebecca Hitchman. Most plain-search results were exhibitor announcements (Powtoon, Trulience, Edplay.ai, Studytube, Mindtools Kineo, etc.) and award / panel voices (Emma Klosson, John Helmer, Jon Torrens) that are speaker / vendor-side rather than attendee-side. Worth knowing for next year: a single “%23LT26UK + I’m + attending” query did most of the heavy lifting.
  • HR Technologies UK is overwhelmingly exhibitor-dominated. Ran 5+ keyword variations (“HR Technologies + attending,” “HR Technologies UK + looking forward / see you,” “%23HRTechUK + I’ll be / I am attending,” “HR Technologies + Excel London + looking forward,” “HR Technologies + Wednesday + ExCeL”). Consistent picture: HRIS / payroll / WFM vendors (Dayforce, IRIS HCM, hireful, IDGateway, In-Workforce, Hono AI, UKG / DuraSuite, Studytube) doing booth-promo posts; almost zero in-house Heads of HR / People posting attendee-style content. Francis Smiley was the cleanest practitioner-leaning attendee voice the searches surfaced.
  • Cross-event signal: Mark Mccartney is the strongest individual find of this sweep — healthcare-leadership coach attending BOTH events on the same day. Healthcare is a Tier 1 industry for Real Talk and dual-event attendance suggests genuine community presence rather than vendor-promo behaviour.
  • One non-attendee worth flagging for the watchlist: David Fagan — Director at HRIT People, “connecting the HR Systems & Transformation Community.” 20 reactions on a substantive WFM-strategy post. Didn’t explicitly say he’s attending HR Tech this week, so not added as a card — but he’s a connector node in the HR systems / transformation community and worth adding to the named-account watchlist for future runs.
  • Skipped (vendor-side or already covered): Bill Paris (Director, Motivait — engagement vendor); Sophie Costin (Make Real, VR vendor); Andy Storch (US keynote speaker); Kirstie Greany (Stand B10 podcast / press); Jon Torrens (communication coach attending to analyse talks); Joe Hirst (exhibition stand builder); John Kostoulas (Dayforce VP — HRIS vendor); Tate Allen (IRIS HCM marketing).
  • Combined day total now: 12 cards across four sections — 4 CHRO · 1 ICP · 7 Influencer. Beyond the 6–9 daily target because of the user-requested ad-hoc sweeps; flagged here for transparency.

Run notes — LT26 attendee sweep

  • Targeted ad-hoc sweep at Toby’s request: find Learning Technologies (LT26UK, 29–30 April, ExCeL London) attendees rather than exhibitors. 4 cards added — all bucketed as CHRO / Path 2 (L&D buyer / champion).
  • Filter applied: excluded vendor / exhibitor announcements (Powtoon, Colossyan, HowNow, Mindtools Kineo, Trulience, Upside Learning, Mahon Fitzgerald / TalentLMS-style, Make Real, Imperagen) and skipped recruiter posts. Kept four clear attendee voices ranging from junior (Genevieve, 30 Under 30) through mid-career (Georgia, Senior Advisor) to senior (Michael, Sr Director leading a panel).
  • Strongest pick: Michael Coates — Sr Director L&D Localization, 7th/8th LT conference, leading a panel, 32 reactions / 7 comments / 3 reposts and explicitly inviting unknown connections to DM him. Best near-term outreach candidate of the four.
  • Highest reach: Genevieve Osborne (45 reactions) and Georgia Hannam (42 reactions) — both warm community posts rather than pitches; comments from Toby will be visible to the L&D community engaging with each post.
  • One borderline-skipped: Andy Storch (keynote speaker / consultant, US-based, attending) — clearly an attendee in the “walking the floor to learn” sense but speaker / vendor-side rather than buyer-side. Skipped to honour the “attendees not exhibitors” brief.
  • One borderline-skipped: Sophie Costin (Director of Learning at Make Real) — framing was attendee-style (“if you see me, say hi”) and the post called out the missing focus on learner experience, but Make Real is a VR/immersive learning vendor so she’s exhibitor-adjacent.
  • Search bias note: “Learning Technologies + attending” is heavily exhibitor-skewed by default. The most useful queries for finding actual attendees were “%23LT26UK + I’m + attending,” “Learning Technologies + ExCeL,” and “LT26 + excited.” Best signal came from posts mentioning specific session titles or the 30 Under 30 cohort.
  • Slugs: all four use the people-search URL pattern. None of these candidates surfaced with a clean “/in/<slug>/” in the search HTML to upgrade.

2026-04-28 — Supplemental run

1. fscom

FCA Skilled Persons Framework 2026–2030 appointment · Lots C (Controls & risk), E (Financial crime), I (Prudential risk) · what s166 actually looks like in practice · very high-engagement compliance-community stage
Influencer TODO
Role
fscom — UK FS regulatory consultancy. Just announced FCA Skilled Persons Framework appointment for 2026–2030 across three lots. Strongest piece in this supplemental sweep — mass-tagged a long list of compliance practitioners (Elaine Tighe, Philip Creed, Jamie Cooke, Alison Donnelly, Nick Owen CBE, etc.), so the comment thread is densely populated with UK FCA-regulated compliance leaders. Classic Track-2 mining ground.
Post topic
Announces fscom’s appointment to the FCA Skilled Persons Framework 2026–2030 across Lot C (controls & risk management), Lot E (financial crime), and Lot I (prudential risk). Useful as Audience C because: (a) high engagement on a regulator-credentialled vendor announcement; (b) the practitioner names tagged in the post are all UK FCA-regulated compliance leaders worth scanning for follow-on Audience B Track-2 mining; (c) the s166 / skilled-person topic is exactly Real Talk’s evidence-of-readiness territory.
Engagement
105 reactions · 22 comments · 11 reposts
Post age
6d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Congrats. The interesting shift in this cycle is that an s166 review is increasingly testing whether your senior managers can demonstrate judgement under pressure, not whether your control framework reads well on paper. Most firms I work with have the framework — the gap is the contemporaneous evidence of how named individuals actually engaged with it. That’s where the reviews tend to bite.

2. Averion Advisory

Section 165 as the “show me” that escalates to Section 166 (“we don’t believe you”) · data, governance, MI consistency, ownership of the response · how firms quietly build the case for their own skilled-person review
Influencer TODO
Role
Averion Advisory — UK FS regulatory consultancy. Smaller voice than fscom but the post itself is genuinely useful and compliance-practitioner pitched.
Post topic
Frames the FCA’s Section 165 information request as the regulator’s last test before deciding whether something more serious is needed — arguing that a poorly-handled Section 165 response is how firms quietly build the case for their own Section 166 skilled-person review. Walks through the failure modes: inconsistent data, MI that doesn’t align with what the board sees, no clear ownership of the response, long explanations that avoid the question. Closes on the “show me” vs “we don’t believe you, so we’ll verify it ourselves” distinction. Direct fit for Real Talk’s evidence-of-readiness positioning. Engagement is low (1 repost) so this is a relationship-start rather than a high-reach stage.
Engagement
0 visible reactions · 0 comments · 1 repost
Post age
5d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The “show me” vs “we don’t believe you” framing is right. The bit firms quietly underestimate is that by the time a Section 165 lands, what the FCA is really probing is whether senior management can answer in the moment with consistent, contemporaneous evidence — not whether the policy library is in order. Most firms can produce documents. Far fewer can produce a person who can talk through them under pressure.

3. Lorna Feeney

6.1m UK line managers untrained for mental health conversations · only 29% of 8.58m managers trained · mental ill-health = #1 cause of long-term absence · HR Grapevine research, Marsh framing
Influencer TODO
Role
Mental Health & Wellbeing Practice Leader at Marsh (global insurance broker, 500+ employees). Vendor / practice leader voice rather than buyer — hence Audience C. UK-focused, citing HR Grapevine research.
Post topic
Cites new HR Grapevine research: 6.1m UK line managers do not have mental health training; only 29% of 8.58m UK managers are trained. Mental ill-health remains the #1 cause of long-term absence and #2 of short-term absence. Argues this is exactly the conversation gap (recognising signs, opening difficult conversations, escalation pathways) that drives stress, disengagement, and avoidable absence. Useful as a stat-rich Audience C piece — the research itself is something Toby could cite in his own posts. The vendor framing is overt (Marsh selling line manager training) but the data is the strongest part.
Engagement
1 reaction · 0 comments · 0 reposts
Post age
~2h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The 29% number is the one HR teams will quietly want to ignore. What we’re seeing with clients is that even within the 29% who’ve been trained, very few can actually open the conversation when the moment lands — it’s a different muscle to the policy walk-through. The gap that matters isn’t training coverage; it’s whether managers have practised the first awkward sentence.

Run notes — 2026-04-28 supplemental

  • Supplemental sweep added 3 more cards. All Audience C / Influencer this time — that’s where the high-engagement, on-message material was. CHRO and ICP practitioner voices were genuinely thin on the second pass too.
  • Strongest pick: fscom’s FCA Skilled Persons Framework appointment — 105 reactions, 22 comments, 11 reposts. The comments are mass-tagged practitioners (Elaine Tighe, Philip Creed, Nick Owen CBE, etc.) — classic Track-2 mining ground if Toby wants to scan the thread for direct compliance-leader follow-ups.
  • Lorna Feeney / Marsh research worth citing: 6.1m UK line managers untrained for mental health conversations, only 29% of 8.58m trained. HR Grapevine source. Useful stat for Toby’s own posts independent of whether he comments here.
  • Searches that came up dry: “learning transfer / training transfer” (drama-based vendor + Soji canvas, no in-house Heads of L&D), “manager effectiveness data/evidence” (image-only results, no extractable text), “Head of Compliance / CCO + FCA” (recruiter posts only), “Head of Employee Relations / ER casework” (recruiter posts only), “leadership programme + impact/transfer” (off-topic tax pricing + NHS coaching), “first-time manager / accidental manager” (mostly Asia-Pacific + US SMB consultants), “compliance theatre / paper compliance” (off-topic food safety + India Pharma).
  • Search-bias confirmation (supplemental cut): recruiter noise dominates “Head of X” title-keyword searches across Compliance, ER, and L&D — these are higher signal as Track-2 mining queries (find the post, scan the comments) than as direct-discovery queries.
  • Skipped (already covered or recently covered): Browne Jacobson FS & Insurance Apr 27 ABI roundtable promotion (different angle to Apr 23/26-b but firm already over-indexed); Neil Makwana Year 2 Consumer Duty piece (already covered Apr 24); Chris Syder ERA franchise piece (already covered Apr 23).

2026-04-28 — Daily run

1. Greenberg Traurig London

FCA non-financial misconduct regime · bullying & harassment moving from HR to regulatory perimeter · 1 September 2026 · what firms should be doing now
Influencer TODO
Role
Greenberg Traurig London — global Big Law firm. Authors: Matt Hancock, Virginia Allen, Kate Martin, Julia Nefedovica.

Argues the FCA’s new NFM regime (effective 1 September 2026) is a material shift, not a refinement: bullying, harassment and other serious misconduct move firmly inside the regulatory perimeter and out of pure HR territory. Sets out what firms should be doing now to prepare. Strongest Big Law stage in this scan — high engagement, on-message, links to a fuller article. Watchlist hit for the broader UK Big Law cluster (different firm to last week’s Browne Jacobson / Lewis Silkin / Travers Smith pieces).
Engagement
52 reactions · 0 comments · 4 reposts
Post age
4d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The shift firms keep underestimating is that the regulator is no longer asking whether the conduct happened — it’s asking whether the senior manager can evidence what they did to prevent it. Most firms I work with have policies and training records; very few have anything that proves their managers can actually run the conversation under pressure. That’s the gap September 2026 will expose.

Founder, BuzzHR — outsourced HR services for small UK businesses. High-engagement HR-influencer voice, primarily speaking to small employers but the post itself is a stage with 117 reactions and active comments.

Plain-English breakdown of the Employment Rights Act 2025 changes that landed 6 April: SSP from day one, day-one paternity / unpaid parental leave, explicit whistleblowing protection for sexual harassment disclosures, and the new Fair Work Agency with inspection and penalty powers. Frames it as the most significant overhaul of UK employment law in a generation. Useful as Audience C because the comment thread is full of HR practitioners flagging which change is biting hardest in their business — classic Track-2 mining ground if Toby wants to scan replies later.

117 reactions · 11 comments · 0 reposts

~1d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The change most firms are quietly underestimating is the whistleblowing protection for harassment disclosures — it changes how a single complaint can escalate. What we’re seeing with clients is that the policy update lands fine, but most line managers still can’t hold the first conversation when someone raises something difficult. That’s the bit that will get tested before the policy ever does.

3. Nick Snaith

ERA 2025 + frontline manager capability · “forwarded PDF isn’t development” · flexible working day 3, third-party harassment, probation closing · what managers actually do Monday morning
Influencer TODO
Role
L&D Strategist, Founder of The Learning Manager (TLM); ex-National Crime Agency. UK-based; sells management programmes — vendor / consultant, hence Audience C.
Post topic
Bluntly argues that most organisations responded to the Employment Rights Act 2025 by sending a policy email — “a forwarded PDF isn’t development.” Walks through five live changes hitting the frontline manager (day-one unfair dismissal, flexible working from week one, third-party harassment duties, SSP with no waiting days, structured probation) and frames it as a capability gap, not a legal one. Writes in the “what to do Monday morning” voice Real Talk Studio uses. Engagement is modest (4 reactions) but the post is squarely on Real Talk’s pitch and the author has a credible non-vendor backstory (NCA). Worth a comment to start a relationship rather than treat as a high-reach stage.
Engagement
4 reactions · 0 comments · 0 reposts
Post age
~1w
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The “forwarded PDF isn’t development” line is exactly right. What we’re seeing with clients is that frontline managers don’t fall over on the legal change — they fall over on the first awkward sentence: how do you actually open the flexible-working conversation, the probation conversation, the customer-harassment incident? Policies don’t close that gap. Practising the conversation does.

4. Deborah Owen

Performance Improvement Plans as a last resort, not a starting point · manager capability is what comes first · PIPs without prior training “feel like punishment”
CHRO TODO
Role
HR Professional, Chartered MCIPD — 25 years experience. Practitioner voice, UK-based. Path 2 Audience A profile (L&D / manager-development champion) at the strategic-people-manager level. Company not visible from post; treat as practitioner / fractional HR voice rather than confirmed in-house at a 500+ employer.
Post topic
Argues a PIP should never be the start of a performance conversation — it should be a last resort after support, training, and meaningful manager-led conversations have been provided. Lists the questions HR should ask before a PIP: has the person been trained, were expectations clear, have we equipped them, has the manager actually had supportive conversations? Calls out the cost of skipping that step: anxiety, lost trust, attrition of good people. Closes with what high-performing organisations do differently — invest in manager capability, prioritise upskilling, address issues early through coaching conversations. Direct fit with Real Talk Studio’s “build the capability before the procedural step” thesis.
Engagement
12 reactions · 0 comments · 1 repost
Post age
5d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
Agreed — the PIP question is almost always a manager-capability question wearing different clothes. What we’re seeing with clients is that the conversations that would have prevented the PIP six months earlier are exactly the ones managers haven’t been built to hold. The investment that matters is upstream of the formal process, not inside it.

5. David Leech FRSA

ERA 2025 reshapes recruitment, induction and probation · “manager capability becoming mission-critical” · risk: over-engineered processes and risk-averse hiring · opportunity if handled well
CHRO TODO
Role
HR Leader & Charity Trustee, FRSA. Professional Services & Public Sector background; HR Transformation, Performance, Retention & Engagement. PDC accredited Coach. Path 2 Audience A profile at the senior HR / fractional level. UK-based.
Post topic
Frames the Employment Rights Act 2025 as a material shift in how recruitment, induction and early employment have to work — not just another compliance exercise. Day-one rights, sick pay and family leave changes, stronger protections for new starters all raise the stakes. Calls out four practical implications: less margin for error in recruitment, tighter probation and induction, manager capability becoming mission-critical, HR systems needing redesign not just policy updates. Flags the risk of over-engineered processes / risk-averse hiring — and the opportunity if handled well. Engagement is thin but the framing — especially “manager capability becoming mission-critical” — is exactly Real Talk’s territory.
Engagement
1 reaction · 0 comments · 0 reposts
Post age
~17h
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The “manager capability becoming mission-critical” line is the one HR teams will quietly underestimate. What we’re seeing with clients is that the policy and process redesign happens fast — the capability uplift to actually run the new probation and induction conversations under pressure rarely follows. That’s where the tribunal risk and the retention risk both quietly compound.

6. Priscilla Gaudoin FICA

SMCR overhaul: a recalibration not a deregulation · less rigid process, more regulatory discretion · “does making accountability more flexible strengthen the system, or quietly weaken the trust it was designed to rebuild?”
ICP TODO
Role
Head of Risk & Compliance, FICA Certified. Direct ICP — senior compliance practitioner, UK-regulated FS. Posts in clean regulatory vocabulary (HMT, FCA, PRA, accountability frameworks).
Post topic
Argues the UK’s SMCR overhaul is more nuanced than “deregulation” or “simplification” — it’s a recalibration: less rigid process, more regulatory discretion, and a subtle shift in where accountability lives. Closes with the question worth pulling on: does making accountability more flexible strengthen the system, or quietly weaken the trust it was designed to rebuild? Engagement is currently thin (no public count) but the voice and seniority are right and the post is genuine practitioner reflection rather than vendor content. Track-1 ICP candidate worth a thoughtful comment to start a relationship.
Engagement
~0–3 reactions visible · 0 comments · 0 reposts
Post age
5d
Link
Find on LinkedIn
Draft comment
The accountability-vs-discretion question is the right one to sit with. What we’re seeing with clients is that the firms most exposed are the ones whose SMCR maturity was always procedural — once you loosen the process, there’s no underlying judgement muscle to fall back on. The interesting test is whether senior managers can hold the conversation when the framework is less prescriptive, not whether the framework was lighter on paper.

Run notes — 2026-04-28

  • Final shortlist: 6 cards. 2 CHRO · 1 ICP · 3 Influencer. Skewed toward Audience C because that’s where the high-engagement, on-message material was.
  • Strongest pick: Greenberg Traurig London — 52 reactions, 4 reposts on the FCA NFM regime / 1 September 2026 piece. New Big Law entrant for the watchlist (different firm to Browne Jacobson / Lewis Silkin / Travers Smith covered last week). Also a strong Track-2 mining ground if Toby scans the comment thread.
  • Influencer pick worth flagging: Grace Nicholls (BuzzHR) — 117 reactions, 11 comments. SMB voice but the comment section is full of UK HR practitioners reacting to specific ERA 2025 changes; classic Track-2 mining target on a future run.
  • Compliance ICP bucket genuinely thin again. Ran 7+ Track 1 searches (Consumer Duty / vulnerable customers, SMCR conduct rules training, three lines of defence + culture, FCA NFM bullying, Dear CEO FCA, compliance culture, “compliance training not enough”). Most strong content this week is coming from law firms and consultancies (Greenberg Traurig, Browne Jacobson, Sicsic, Pathlight) rather than in-house Heads of Compliance. Priscilla Gaudoin (Head of Risk & Compliance) was the cleanest practitioner voice; engagement is thin so it’s a relationship-start rather than a high-reach stage.
  • CHRO bucket: Path 1 (Compliance/Risk) carried the day, Path 2 (L&D) was the weaker half. “Knowing-doing gap” returned non-UK content (US nursing, Pakistani public health, Australian construction). “Manager capability + Head of L&D” returned a vendor (LeaderCoreAI — direct competitor, low engagement). “Difficult conversations + line manager training” surfaced Marsh + a vendor-as-CEO L&D pitch, no in-house Head of L&D voices. Worth rotating Path 2 picks next run — try “learning transfer,” “leader as coach,” or “manager effectiveness data” instead.
  • Toby’s own NFM/COCON post surfaced in the “non-financial misconduct + COCON” search (~20h, 2 comments). Useful only as a signal that the keyword is sparse this week — one good practitioner post would surface near the top.
  • Watchlist hits this run: Greenberg Traurig London (Big Law), City HR Association (CHRO benchmarking survey, low signal but worth keeping). Lewis Silkin search returned an A.I.M™ vendor and an EBRD job ad — no on-topic Lewis Silkin content this week.
  • Solution-aware (Path 3) was quiet: “AI roleplay + leadership/learning” surfaced LinkedIn Learning event content (3w old, 93 reactions but stale) and VirtualSpeech case study (UK psychology students — on-topic but already not a Toby fit). LeaderCoreAI vendor content present but not engagement-rich enough.
  • Status-quo (Path 4) returned medium signal: “Tick box + compliance training” pulled Venu Prasad Menon (Amnet, 30 reactions, global voice) and Colossyan vendor content. “Completion rates + meaningful/enough” surfaced Tracie Sponenberg again (already covered Apr 26 and not on-topic this time).

  • Profile slugs: all six cards use the LinkedIn people-search URL pattern (name + role/firm keyword) per skill default. None of these candidates surfaced with a clean “/in/<slug>/” in the search HTML to upgrade.

2026-04-27 — Daily run

Notes a Lexarius AI-powered roleplay demo as “seriously impressive” for capability building. Solution-aware Path 3 candidate — already thinking in the AI-roleplay / behavioural-practice category. ~1d
